data.title: Green energy giant admits a quarter of its wind power is being wasted
data.description:
Nearly a quarter of the wind power generated by one of Britain’s biggest electricity companies is being wasted because of grid congestion.

SSE, which controls part of Scotland’s transmission network and multiple wind farms, has admitted that 23pc of the output from its onshore and offshore wind farms was “constrained” in the six months to the end of September.

This means it was paid to switch off or curtail its turbines because the UK electricity grid could not cope with the power they were generating. The costs of the curtailment were added to energy bills.

The disclosure came in the company’s financial results for the six months to September, in which SSE also warned it will fall far short of its own target for renewable energy generation.

The company said: “Given the challenging market and policy environment, SSE is unlikely to meet its ambitious goal of 50 terawatt-hours (TWh) of renewable generation output by 2030.”

Last year, SSE managed only 13 TWh of renewable output, with another 5.4 TWh in the six months to September.

The update will be a major blow to Ed Miliband’s hopes of decarbonising the UK’s electricity system by 2030.

Grid capacity is a key reason why SSE is struggling to hit its target. Wind farms are paid to switch off – a practice known as constraint or curtailment by the industry – when the electricity grid is too congested to handle their power.

Households and businesses ultimately bear constraint costs through their energy bills, which have been rising for years. The overall costs have exceeded £1.24bn already this year, according to the tracker website Wasted Wind, compared to £1.23bn for the whole of 2024.

SSE controls major developments, including the Seagreen Offshore Wind Farm, off Scotland’s east coast, and the Viking Wind Farm in the Shetland Islands.

The company has been cutting back on its wider green commitments. Earlier this year SSE announced a £3bn reduction in its investments in renewables, blaming planning and policy delays by the UK and Scottish governments. It also cut 300 jobs from its green energy business.

It remains a mainly fossil-fuel-based power generator, with over half of its electricity coming from gas-fired power stations.

As well as generating power, SSE also runs the high-voltage grid in northern Scotland and lower-voltage networks linking homes and businesses to the national grid across central southern England and the north of Scotland.

On Wednesday, the company announced £33bn of investments, much of it targeted at those networks. The company said the plan would “help to eliminate constraints within SSE’s transmission network”.

A spokesman said: “Constraints happen when the energy being produced in one part of the country can’t get to another because there simply isn’t the grid capacity to carry it.

“The constraint of any operational energy asset is a decision for the National Electricity System Operator, which is solely responsible for the design and operation of the constraint payment system, as well as decisions regarding which assets are curtailed. These responsibilities do not lie with individual companies.”

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said SSE’s decision to invest £33bn was a “vote of confidence” in Ed Miliband’s clean power mission, which would cut constraint costs “and help bring down bills for households for good”.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Thanks to Ed Miliband’s ridiculous net zero plans the cost of paying wind farms to switch off when it’s too windy is set to reach £8bn by 2030 – and all of those costs get added to up on people’s bills. Labour need to focus on making energy cheap.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The Cop30 summit won’t save us but technology just might
data.description:
Let us start with a stiff whisky and the bad news. The state of the planet on the eve of Cop30 in Brazil is abominable.

Concentrations of carbon, methane and nitrous oxide – the three main greenhouse gases – all reached record levels last year.

The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says CO2 emissions rose by 3.5 parts per million to 423.9, the largest one-year rise in observable history.

Aerosol pollution repressed the latent warming for a long time but tighter controls on sulfur and dirty shipping are now unleashing it.

The WMO said oceans were the warmest ever recorded in 2024 and the temperature is rising at an accelerating rate. They are absorbing 90pc of surplus heat energy but soak up less carbon as they warm. It is a vicious circle.

The Global Forest Review says the tropics lost a record 6.7 million hectares of primary rainforest in 2024. The loss rate of world tree-cover has doubled since the early 2000s and is increasingly driven by fire, above all in the boreal forests of Canada and Russia.

Hotter temperatures and longer droughts turn the wood into kindling. The fires release stored CO2 from both the trees and the soil. Welcome to the “fire-climate feedback loop”.

The WMO said peak winter sea ice in the Arctic was the lowest ever at 13.8 million square kilometres in 2024. Open water absorbs more solar heat, quickening the albedo feedback loop.

The Humboldt glacier in Venezuela is no more. Last year was the worst yet recorded for net glacier loss, hitting all 19 regions of the globe and accounting for a fifth of rising sea levels. The last glaciers on Kilimanjaro and Indonesia’s Puncak Jaya are disappearing.

The good news is less obvious but visible in the “second derivative” – to borrow the term used by hedge funds to spot early signs of a trend change.

One sector is toppling after another. The think tank Ember said global fossil use in industry peaked in 2014, thanks to the electrification of light manufacturing in China.

Fossil emissions from textiles, machinery, wood products, pulp and paper, and food processing are all declining in absolute terms. Chemicals, steel and agriculture have peaked and plateaued.

Sales of petrol and diesel cars peaked in 2017. Fossil use in buildings peaked in 2018. Global transport has finally begun to roll over, as you would expect since electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids have surpassed 50pc of car sales in China and even 40-ton lorries are going green.

China’s industrial revolution is over. It is now a “mature” economy in demographic contraction with an overbuilt housing stock and swaths of manufacturing overcapacity.

That matters infinitely more than culture war disputes in Britain or Germany over the pace of net zero.

China has driven 80pc of net fossil demand growth this century and accounts for a third of global emissions – as much as the US and the EU combined.

The surprise development is that China’s coal use already seems to have peaked. The country reached its goal of 1,200 gigawatts (GW) of wind and solar six years early in 2024. Its CO2 emissions have dropped by around 1pc so far this year.

Electrotech is becoming unstoppable in countries hosting 80pc of humanity that are net importers of fossil energy.

They have no economic interest in remaining dependent on constant shipments from petrostates, bleeding their capital accounts for decades to come.

They have better alternatives. Solar plus batteries plus digital tech operating together are now the unbeatable trio in sub-40 latitudes where most people live.

Pakistan’s solar rush slays every myth of the fossil lobby. The country imported 17GW of solar panels last year alone. This had nothing to do with state activism or climate policy.

Poor farmers are installing solar because the price of a 1kW panel from China has collapsed to £100 and undercuts diesel generators.

Small factory owners are installing solar and batteries because they are the cheapest source of power and because the rickety grid is prone to blackouts. The switch is driven by the price signal and ordinary people.

Sales of EVs have reached 76pc of the market in Nepal. They are cheap. A BAW Brumby retails for £10,800 and a Wuling Air for £13,000.

The Nepalese government is helping this along and for good reason. Smog is unbearable in Kathmandu. But also because the country has abundant hydro-electric power. Ethiopia is going the same route for the same reason.

Global capex in electrotech is $2tn (£1.5tn) a year. The figure is $1tn for fossil energy. Finance has already moved on.

“All of the net growth in global electricity generation in the first half of 2025 came from solar and wind for the first time,” said Ember’s Kingsmill Bond.

Old coal and gas plants will not be replaced one for one as they retire. New solar farms will increasingly take over on pure cost, even in the Persian Gulf. Abu Dhabi has just broken ground on a giga-scale solar park that will produce 1GW of 24/7 baseload power with batteries at $60 per MWh.

Do not be fooled by the old canard that 80pc of “primary energy demand” still comes from fossils and that this figure has barely changed, while renewables merely add a little on the side.

It is akin to arguing in 1920 that coal-powered ships were the future because (more efficient) oil engines were still just 2pc of maritime shipping. We know in hindsight that the death spiral was already underway. The quick-witted see the rate of change. The slow-witted see only the stock.

It is the final energy used that matters. The International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis says the fossil share on that metric has dropped to 66pc and is on an accelerating downward glide-path.

The critical point is that fossil molecules waste two thirds of their energy in heat when burnt in power plants. Petrol cars waste three quarters. Renewable electrons do not.

Compare an old light bulb powered by a gas plant with an LED bulb (eight times more efficient) powered off wind. The primary energy required is circa 20 times less for the same illumination. You can extend that principle – with varying ratios – to EVs, heat pumps, electric arc steel or plasma-heated cement kilns.

Some of the cleverest minds in global energy analysis now think that a richer world will need only half as much energy supply in the future thanks to the marvels of technology, even allowing for data centres and rising living standards in the Global South.

Whether we will reach this happy state fast enough to beat the awaiting climate feedback loops is the open question, which takes us back to China. Xi Jinping is not retreating. He is going for broke on global electrotech supremacy.

China is targeting 3,600GW of wind and solar by 2035. The Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA) thinks the country will surpass this and could reach 4,500GW. These are staggering figures and imply a drastic fall in coal combustion.

As always with China, there is a catch. It is using more coal to make synthetic fuels and chemicals. It is planning more coal-fired plants, typically as a back-up for renewables – but many may never be built and the remainder will lie mostly idle with falling usage rates.

The real debate is whether China will plateau for a few years or whether it is closer to a cliff-edge fall in emissions. On this hangs our fate.

China is not decarbonising to save the planet. It aims a) to outflank the West and win the global energy contest, and b) to free China from dependence on seaborne fossil fuels that are vulnerable to a US naval blockade. Cop30 is irrelevant to these calculations.

The Paris climate accord in 2015 was a game-changer but we are now a decade on. Electrotech has since won the argument on both cost and energy security.

We may or we may not avert a scorching runaway world of two degrees plus, but whether we succeed will have nothing to do with anything said or agreed by 50,000 people descending on Belém. It will be decided by geopolitics, market prices and the tidal force of technological change.

data.title: The BBC will need to change to win back the trust of women
data.description:
I thought it right and proper that Tim Davie resign over impartiality failures, but when I posted this on social media, I was immediately accused of being a Trump supporter. As a Left-wing lesbian feminist, I found this both amusing and irritating. We are living in polarised times.

The vast majority of those railing against the bias in coverage of gender issues are feminists on the Left. The BBC’s coverage on gender ideology is the starkest example of bias. Stories – whether in the news, in documentaries or in dramas – ignore reasonable concerns, including those expressed by lesbians and gays, and parents. BBC output on this issue is top heavy with drag queens and happy trans people, with no mention of the negatives.

This is not a new problem. Take the CBBC documentary, I Am Leo, which aired on the BBC channel aimed at children aged 6-12. First shown in November 2014, and repeated in 2015 and 2016, it is a wholly positive portrayal of a young girl transitioning to live as a boy, featuring total affirmation.

CBBC is the leading children’s broadcaster in the UK, reaching 58 per cent of 6-12 year olds. To my knowledge, it has not commissioned a recent documentary programme about a gay or lesbian young person, and seems happy to send the message to children that if you feel in any way “different” to other kids of your sex, this means you are probably transgender.

The Leo documentary promoted regressive attitudes about girls having “pink brains” and boys having blue ones, alongside unchallenged assertions that some children are “trapped in the wrong body” and that transitioning is straightforward and unproblematic. We are told, by a former Tavistock clinic head, that puberty blockers are fully reversible, and simply allow the child “time to think”.

What the BBC might have commissioned, in the interest of balance, was something along the lines of my podcast series, Julie in Genderland, which explores what happens to parents desperately trying to safeguard their children from social and medical transition. All of the parents I interviewed had children who had been “affirmed” in their “gender identity” by teachers and social workers, just as “Leo” was.

Many were told it was a toss-up between allowing their child to transition or risking them killing themselves.

Today, Deborah Turness said that BBC News is not institutionally biased, despite the fact that many important stories have not been covered. As this newspaper reported, the LGBT desk – which is shared by all the Corporation’s news programmes – has been “captured by a small group of people” who are promoting a pro-trans agenda and “keeping other perspectives off air”.

How did the BBC come to be so captured? The BBC Pride Network claims to “raise awareness of upcoming LGBTQ+ related content across the BBC”, and “represent BBC Pride members and all LGBTQ+ people on initiatives to improve diversity amongst staff and content produced for our license fee payers”. But where is the coverage of the many lesbians and gay men, such as those represented by the LGB Alliance, who are concerned about this assumption that every gender non-conforming youngster is transgender, rather than simply same-sex attracted – as is much more likely?

Then there is the in-house training, paid for by the licence fee. In 2022 staff were told, by trans activist group Global Butterflies, that there are more than 150 genders, and to develop their “trans brand” by declaring their pronouns on email sign-offs.

But perhaps the worst failure of the BBC is in its reporting of sexual and violent crimes committed by men that self-identify as women. There is no legal or journalistic requirement in the UK to refer to these men as women, yet headlines such as, “A woman obsessed with death who once livestreamed the killing of a cat has been found guilty of murdering a man,” are regularly posted on its news website, and include reports involving indecent exposure, the sexual assault of children, and rape (a crime that, by law, can only be committed by a man).

The resignations of Davie may be a turning point in the BBC edging back towards its core values of fairness and impartiality. But failure to get its house in order now will be the death of it.

data.title: France is shocked by the fall of the BBC
data.description:
The scandal that has engulfed the BBC has received widespread coverage in France. Television, radio and newspapers have reported on the resignations of director general Tim Davie and Deborah Turness, the head of news, and why they went.

The most comprehensive coverage was in the centre-Right Le Figaro newspaper. Explaining to its readers how the BBC had “manipulated” a speech by Donald Trump, the paper then listed some of the Corporation’s other faux-pas in recent years, name-checking along the way Gary Lineker, Huw Edwards, Hamas and Bob Vylan’s appearance at Glastonbury.

“The image of the institution long cherished by the British, who affectionately refer to it as ‘Auntie,’ has been seriously tarnished,” wrote Le Figaro.

Not just in Britain. France is shocked by what The Telegraph has exposed. Outside Britain, no other country has an historical attachment to the BBC as strong as France. During the Second World War, the Corporation was known as “the Voice of Free France” through its programme, Radio London. The BBC was the medium through which General Charles de Gaulle, exiled in London from 1940 to 1944, spoke to his people during the dark years of Nazi occupation. “Free France exists, in the eyes of the French, only through radio,” said de Gaulle.

Even today, in museums dedicated to those years, the BBC is praised for its role in helping keep alive the spirit of Free France. The historian Aurélie Luneau has written a book called Radio London: The Voice of Liberty 1940-44, in part inspired by the memories of her own family. “The older members of the family spoke of the BBC with almost sentimental fondness,” she explained. “A happy memory of what were nevertheless difficult years.”

The respect felt by the French for the BBC continued long after the war. It was the blue riband of broadcasters, envied as well as feted for the quality and reach of its reporting.

In 1999 Le Monde newspaper described the BBC as “not only the most famous but also the best public radio and television broadcaster on the planet.” It made its claim as an aside while reporting on the controversial appointment as director-general of Greg Dyke.

Dyke got the job despite the fact he had given gifts worth £50,000 to the Labour Party before its victory in the 1997 general election.

That row is innocuous compared to the crisis the Corporation now faces. In 1999, Conservatives worried that Dyke might not be impartial; today only the BBC’s most one-eyed supporters believe it is balanced in its political and cultural outlook. Most agree with Reform leader Nigel Farage that the “BBC has been institutionally biased for decades”.

The role of The Daily Telegraph in holding the BBC to account has also featured prominently in French reporting. One of two conservative commentators have wistfully remarked that if only there was a newspaper of similar character in France to scrutinise state-owned television and radio stations.

Accusations of Left-wing bias have plagued them for as long as they have the BBC. In 2024 the head of France Inter – comparable to Radio 4 – boasted: “France Inter is a progressive radio station and I embrace that.”

During one week in February last year an inventory was made of the political leanings of people invited onto public television and radio stations. Of the 630 guests, 330 were considered neutral, 273 progressive and 27 were conservative.

Any attempt to suggest that public service broadcasting in France has lost its impartiality is met with a furious response. Delphine Ernotte Cunci, the head of France Televisions, recently accused her independent rivals of being “far-Right”. Yet in 2023 Ernotte Cunci told a parliamentary inquiry committee that public television was “trying to represent France as we would like it to be.”

One of the broadcasters Ernotte Cunci regards as “far-Right” is the radio station Europe 1; it is not far-Right, but it is owned by the conservative media tycoon Vincent Bolloré, the bête noire of French progressives.

In an editorial this week Europe 1 committed an act of heresy: it tore apart the sainted BBC. It accused the Corporation of “presenting information as it wanted it to be, rather than as it is…it is a hijacking of the public information space.”

The BBC’s voice is fading in France.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US attacks Starmer over nuclear reactor snub
data.description:
The US has launched an attack on Sir Keir Starmer after he snubbed an American power company’s plans to build a nuclear power station in Wales.

In a rare public rebuke, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador to Britain, hit out at the “extremely disappointing” decision – which effectively prevents the American nuclear giant Westinghouse from building the plant at Wylfa in Anglesey.

Behind the scenes, Westinghouse’s plans have been supported by the Trump administration including officials from the US state department and the department of commerce.

However, the Prime Minister will confirm on Thursday that the country’s first small modular reactors (SMRs), designed by Rolls-Royce and backed by Qatar, will be built at Westinghouse’s preferred site instead.

On Wednesday, a state department source said London’s decision had angered Trump officials, who were offended that they were given little warning that it was coming from either Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, or Sir Keir.

Writing for the Telegraph last month, Mr Stephens urged ministers to “capitalise on the momentum” of Donald Trump’s state visit to the UK by reserving Wylfa for a large nuclear plant of the kind proposed by Westinghouse.

On Wednesday, one well-placed source said: “The Americans are now very p----- off.”

In a statement issued to the press, Mr Stephens said: “We are extremely disappointed by this decision, not least because there are cheaper, faster and already-approved options to provide clean, safe energy at this same location.

“If you want to get shovels in the ground as soon as possible and take a big step in addressing energy prices and availability, there is a different path, and we look forward to decisions soon on large scale nuclear projects.

“As I have repeatedly said, we want the UK to be the strongest possible ally to the US, and high energy costs are an impediment to that.”

A second US state department source said: “This discussion has been long and intense with broad US/UK interests at stake - not just one company.”

In a reference to the UK government’s failure to forewarn their US counterparts about the decision, they added: “There is disappointment not just at the decision but also at how it was conveyed.”

Ministers had already chosen Derby-based Rolls-Royce to design the country’s first SMRs but are yet to officially confirm where they will be built.

However, reports that the Government is poised to confirm Wylfa as the location follow months of lobbying by rival nuclear companies interested in developing power plants in Anglesey.

Rolls-Royce’s SMR business is backed by a Qatari investment fund, US energy provider Constellation, French investment group BNF Resources and ČEZ, a Czech state energy company.

Two sites were potentially in the frame for the mini reactors: Wylfa in Anglesey and Oldbury in Gloucestershire.

Government ‘chose to get going’

Rolls has expressed a preference for Wylfa, which is larger and better-understood because of previous but ultimately abandoned plans to build another plant there.

But Westinghouse had argued privately that ministers should give Oldbury to Rolls and keep Wylfa reserved for a larger, “gigawatt-scale” nuclear project of the kind it was proposing.

It had assembled a consortium behind the project and claimed it could be delivered on similar timescales and with private financing. But the company also wanted £50m for a feasibility study and more time to draw up the proposals.

Ultimately, the Government is thought to have sided with Rolls out of the belief that its SMRs can be built more quickly.

This was partly because EDF, the French nuclear giant behind the under-construction Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C plants, was also expected to be interested in building a large plant at Wylfa.

As a result, officials believed they would have needed to carry out a competitive bidding process potentially lasting for another two years. An EDF insider denied the company would have objected to Westinghouse’s plans.

On Thursday, the Government announced Wylfa had been chosen following months of lobbying by rival nuclear companies interested in developing power plants in Anglesey.

Under the agreement, up to three Rolls-Royce--designed SMRs - enough to power around 3 million homes - will be built on the Welsh island by the mid-2030s.

More could then follow later, with officials confident there is space for up to eight on the site overall. 

Great British Energy Nuclear, a government quango, is overseeing the project, which is slated to create around 3,000 jobs.

Energy secretary Ed Miliband, writing for The Telegraph, said: “The project will be built by publicly owned Great British Energy-Nuclear in partnership with Rolls-Royce SMR ... Nuclear is at the heart of our drive for energy sovereignty and abundance. And as Wylfa’s history shows, the industry has long been a source of well-paid, highly skilled, unionised jobs.”

The announcement was hailed as “national renewal in action” by the PM, who has pledged to “use all the tools in our armoury” to ensure a new generation of nuclear power plants get built.

An industry insider said: “The Government chose to get going. This decision means you can get spades in the ground next year, rather than talking for another five.”

However, a supporter of the Westinghouse bid disputed that argument, saying the company’s AP1000 reactor was tried and tested in the US and China and could be built more quickly than the Rolls design, which is still being finalised.

The Government was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical giant declares war on Brussels over Chinese dumping
data.description:
Billionaire Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s Ineos has stepped up its fight against cheap Chinese imports by launching 10 anti-dumping cases at the European Commission.

The chemicals giant is asking Brussels to slap hefty tariffs on cut-price competition from China, where rival manufacturers enjoy much cheaper energy bills and lower carbon costs.

It has also called for similar measures on imports from the US, the Middle East and elsewhere in Asia.

“Sites are closing, carbon-heavy imports are surging and politicians are still asleep at the wheel,” said Tom Crotty, Ineos group director.

“Unless Europe wakes up fast, it won’t just lose its chemical industry – it will lose the foundation of its entire manufacturing sector.”

The EU’s imports of Chinese chemicals surged 8.3pc in the first half of this year.

Ineos says the European industry is “drowning” in cheap imports and is at imminent risk of factory closures and job losses.

It has also warned of a similar threat to Britain. It has already slashed one in five jobs at its Acetyls chemicals plant in Hull this year, blaming Chinese dumping.

The company’s Olefins and Polymers plant at Grangemouth, in Scotland, which makes products used by hundreds of UK plastic companies, is also under threat from Britain’s high energy prices and carbon taxes.

Ineos added that the EU-US trade deal would throw open the door to more American imports, with European manufacturers paying three times more for gas than American producers.

Sir Jim is looking to protect jobs and production at 15 Ineos sites across Europe, which supply chemicals to industries ranging from cars and construction to packaging, defence, electronics, pharmaceuticals and medical devices.

Ineos has warned that its German-based production of the chemical BDO – used to make antibiotics, antiretrovirals, statins and vitamin B6 – “has no future” unless Brussels blocks Chinese imports made in Xinjiang Province using coal-fired energy.

The company has urged Brussels to take action by the end of this year – but Sir Jim may struggle to get the speed and scale of response he has demanded.

The European Commission’s review of an anti-dumping application can take up to 15 months. Officials in Brussels are reportedly struggling under a flood of applications this year.

The company had already sought anti-dumping protection for its production of the chemical ABS, telling Brussels that its six plants were suffering losses equivalent to two thirds of their normal profitability.

But Brussels proposed an anti-dumping levy of just 3.7pc. Steve Harrington, the Ineos Styrolution boss, described this rate as “completely ineffective” and branded it an act of “industrial self-harm”.

“Unless Europe acts decisively, we are finished,” he said.

European manufacturers must buy emission permits to offset their use of greenhouse gases in production.

The EU has introduced a carbon border tax, or CBAM, which is supposed to level the playing field between European producers and their overseas rivals.

But an Ineos spokesman said: “CBAM is too complex, too narrow and far too slow to make any real difference.

“It won’t save Europe’s industries, it’ll bury them in paperwork while plants shut down.”

Ineos last month released a study commissioned from Oxford Economics which found that 21 major European chemical plants were in the process of shutting.

The €700bn (£614bn) industry is Europe’s fourth-largest and employs 1.2 million people.

Industry figures showed that European chemicals production shrank 2.4pc in the first half of 2025 from the same period a year earlier, while Chinese factories expanded almost 8pc and American output grew 2.6pc.

The European Commission was contacted for comment.

data.title: M&S hires BA boss after chairman attacked airline’s service
data.description:
Marks & Spencer has appointed the boss of British Airways to its board after the retailer’s chairman criticised the airline’s declining service.

Sean Doyle will join M&S next month as a non-executive director to bolster its board in the wake of a damaging cyber attack.

M&S described Mr Doyle as an “outstanding business leader”, adding: “He also represents an iconic British brand with extraordinary public exposure.”

The appointment comes 18 months after Archie Norman, M&S’s chairman, hit out at British Airways for what he claimed was a decline in standards.

In now-deleted posts on X in May last year, Mr Norman said his flight had been “cancelled then delayed” for the third time in the year so far. He said it was “sad to see a great airline deteriorate”.

Mr Norman told The Telegraph: “I decided that, rather than have to post on X when my plane is delayed, we should have Sean on our board so we can feed back directly!

“Both our businesses have trusted brands and complicated customer service issues so there is a lot we can learn from each other.”

Mr Doyle said: “Being the chief executive of Britain’s flag carrier is a very special job, as you are the custodian of a brand which is part of the fabric of the country. Not many brands can equal that but M&S certainly can.”

The appointment follows the hiring of Roger Burnley, the former chief executive of Asda, who will also join M&S’s board next month.

Mr Norman’s tenure as chairman has also been extended until 2029 after shareholders voted for him to stay on beyond the maximum nine-year term recommended under the UK corporate governance code, which he was due to reach next year.

The retail giant is hoping to continue a turnaround that has seen a revival of its fortunes in both its food and fashion divisions.

M&S rejoined the FTSE 100 in 2023 after a four-year absence and its shares have tripled over the last five years.

However, the company was dealt a major setback earlier this year when it was hit by a cyber attack that left shelves empty and brought online sales to a halt. Bosses estimated that the incident would lead to a £300m hit to profits.

BA suffered its own costly cyber attack in 2018, with the airline fined £20m after 400,000 customers had data stolen. Mr Doyle was named chief executive of the airline in 2020.

data.title: Energy giant Ovo slashes costs in survival plan
data.description:
Ovo Energy is preparing to slash tens of millions of pounds in costs under a radical plan to secure its survival.

In a bid to convince the regulator of its financial viability, Britain’s fourth-largest gas and electricity supplier is plotting deep spending cuts to areas including advertising and brand building activities.

The cuts come as Stephen Fitzpatrick, Ovo’s founder, scrambles to meet tougher financial rules imposed by Ofgem.

Under the energy regulator’s new regime, introduced after dozens of suppliers collapsed during the energy crisis in 2022, Britain’s biggest suppliers are required to hold a certain level of cash based on how many customers they have.

For Ovo, which serves four million customers, this means a bill amounting to hundreds of millions of pounds.

The burden of the energy regulator’s rules has been blamed for disrupting Ovo’s attempts to raise £300m in new investment.

Bankers at Rothschild have been tasked with overseeing the fundraising process, with industry sources claiming that some of the UK’s biggest energy suppliers have been offered the chance to invest.

However, potential new investors have so far shunned Ovo over its ongoing regulatory struggles with Ofgem, casting doubt over the company’s future. It warned in its latest accounts that a “material uncertainty” clouded its future.

A lack of new funding has prompted Ovo to instead slash costs to meet its cash requirements. The cuts raise questions over the company’s high-profile sponsorship deals, such as the Wembley Ovo Arena in London.

Details of the new plan have emerged after David Buttress and James Davies stepped down as Ovo’s chief executive and chief financial officer respectively last week.

It is understood that Ofgem and Ovo are in active talks to approve the company’s latest business plan – which is now being overseen by Chris Houghton, who replaced Mr Buttress as chief executive, and chairman Dame Jayne-Anne Gadhia, the former boss of Virgin Money.

Dame Jayne was appointed in July following the departure of Justin King, the ex-Sainsbury’s boss.

The Telegraph can also reveal that Mayfair Equity Partners, Ovo’s second-largest backer, is looking to sell its 30pc stake in the business. The London-based private equity firm first invested in Ovo in 2015, six years after Mr Fitzpatrick founded the company.

Ovo has been chasing fresh investment for months, but has so far struggled to strike a deal. It emerged earlier this year that Iberdrola, the owner of Scottish Power, had held talks with Ovo about a potential merger, while Verdane, a Norwegian investment group, recently abandoned a potential investment.

Ovo supplies gas and electricity to four million customers across the UK, making it an integral part of the country’s energy system.

While there is no immediate threat to the company’s future, the energy sector has been hit by the collapse of two smaller suppliers in recent months, underlining the continued peril in the industry.

Rising wholesale energy costs were blamed for the failure of Rebel Energy in April, while Tomato Energy was plunged into administration last week. Combined, the two failures affected more than 100,000 customers.

In a possible effort to improve its finances, Ovo last month announced plans to double costs for electric vehicle customers.

Under a controversial new deal, drivers will now have to pay 14p a kilowatt hour (kWh) to charge their electric vehicles, up from 7p per kWh previously. Customers will only be exempt if they sign up to a new monthly plan worth a minimum of £27.50.

The proposal sparked a barrage of criticism from customers online. One driver said: “This is catastrophic. I assume Ovo just wants to get rid of their EV customers.”

Ovo denied that financial pressures had prompted the tariff change, instead claiming it was in response to customer requests for a simpler payment system.

Mr Fitzpatrick founded Ovo in 2009 and owns the business alongside Kensington Roof Gardens, a glitzy private members’ club in west London once owned by Sir Richard Branson.

Despite recording losses of £135m in 2024 and warning over its ability to stay afloat, the company still approved £27m in payments to a company owned by Mr Fitzpatrick last year.

He has extracted tens of millions of pounds from Ovo through brand royalty fees in recent years, although these payments have now been curbed under Ofgem’s new financial rules.

A spokesman for Mayfair Equity Partners said: “Mayfair Equity Partners is a long-term supporter and shareholder of Ovo. We continue to have full confidence in Ovo’s leadership team and its strategy to build a sustainable energy future.”

Ovo declined to comment and Ofgem were contacted for comment.

data.title: Wind farm subsidy cuts will push up energy bills, says green investment giant
data.description:
Cuts to wind and solar farm subsidies will push up household bills, one of Britain’s biggest green investment funds has claimed.

Greencoat UK Wind on Tuesday warned that proposed changes to the way the Government calculates support for renewable projects would “inevitably erode investor confidence”.

This would cause lenders to view UK projects as riskier and demand higher returns on their capital, which Greencoat claims would “substantially” increase costs for households and businesses, who ultimately pay for new energy infrastructure.

On Tuesday, the Government said it “did not recognise” the claims.

Greencoat’s objections relate to the Renewables Obligation (RO), a subsidy that was closed to new entrants in 2017 but has become one of the biggest levies on consumer bills.

For a household paying about £958 per year for electricity, around £102 goes towards paying for the RO.

The scheme was designed to incentivise the rollout of green energy.

It allows wind and solar farms to top up their income from electricity generation by issuing them with certificates, which they also sell to energy suppliers.

The minimum “floor” price of these certificates is set by the Government and increases in line with the Retail Prices Index (RPI) – a measure of inflation which delivers higher increases than the Consumer Prices Index (CPI).

But in a recent consultation, the Energy Department proposed switching the RO scheme to CPI, arguing the RPI link had handed generators overly generous returns and the shift would reduce the burden on consumers.

It said: “To address concerns around overcompensation and ensure a fairer inflation adjustment mechanism for the RO, the UK Government ... [is] considering two options for transitioning from the Retail Price Index (RPI) to the Consumer Price Index (CPI).

“Both options aim to deliver a more proportionate approach to inflation indexation, reduce costs to consumers and align with broader government and regulatory policy.”

The first option would see the scheme immediately switch to CPI inflation.

The second would freeze the RO floor price at current levels, then only begin increasing it again once the backdated CPI price surpassed that figure.

According to the Government, the second option would result in annual savings for consumers of £820m by 2031-32.

But renewable developers have hit out against the proposals.

Greencoat said: “Investors have made good faith investments into UK renewable energy projects based on stable, government-backed, inflation-linked support.

“Retrospective revision to the RO will inevitably erode investor confidence.

“We anticipate that investors will demand a higher return on new investments to compensate for the risk of further government intervention.

“A small increase in the cost of capital would substantially increase the cost to consumers of new renewable energy projects and can reasonably be expected to outweigh the purported savings to consumers and so serve to increase, rather than decrease, bills.”

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “We do not recognise these claims.

“By reforming legacy schemes, we’re delivering better value for bill payers – saving millions while supporting lower energy bills for energy-intensive companies.

“This supports our clean energy mission, which will get us off the roller-coaster of fossil fuel prices and provide businesses and households with lower bills for good.”

data.title: Nobody’s buying London’s shiny new homes
data.description:
They are supposed to be the solution to London’s housing crisis – but new-build properties in the capital are increasingly unloved by buyers.

Construction of new homes in the capital has ground to a halt and the situation looks unlikely to change.

Developers are abandoning building sites and just 6pc of the 176,000 homes promised by Sir Sadiq Khan in the next two years are likely to be built, according to consultancy Molior.

Everything from onerous red tape to Sir Sadiq’s mandate that over a third of new housing be sold at a discount has been blamed for the slowdown.

But one factor stifling the city’s supply of new-build homes has arguably been overlooked: almost nobody is buying them.

The latest available figures for the first six months of the year show that only 3,946 new private housing units changed hands in London, down 30pc compared with the same period in 2024, and the lowest level since the global financial crisis.

The decline is far more drastic when compared with sales of housing overall in the city, which dropped by about 1pc compared with last year, analysis by estate agents Hamptons shows.

Housing has become unaffordable

One big factor behind the market slump is the change to property tax.

Would-be homeowners in the capital were hit hard when the tax-free stamp duty allowance was halved in April from £250,000 to £125,000. In addition, the nil rate threshold for first-time buyers fell from £425,000 to £300,000, costing them an additional £7,000 on average.

Sales of new-build property in particular saw a significant hit a month later. Just 19 new flat sales were processed across the whole city in May, according to data from the Land Registry.

The figures are likely to be revised upwards in the coming years, but suggest transactions of new housing in the capital have declined significantly.

As London developers struggle to shift new housing stock in the face of weak demand, plans to build thousands more new properties have grown uncertain amid fears they simply will not sell.

Neal Hudson, of housing market research firm Built Place, explained how Britain’s acute housing shortage has failed to produce an excess of eager buyers in London.

“You need to sell flats upfront to unlock the finance to get them built,” he says. Without this, developers face the risk that buyers who are willing to pay a premium for a new home won’t materialise, and so being left with unsold stock.

Buyer sentiment has also been affected by higher interest rates in the aftermath of Liz Truss’s mini-Budget in 2022 which has made mortgages unaffordable, Hudson says.

If an average worker in London wants to buy a home, the average purchase price is more than 13 times their salary, compared with eight times the average UK salary for homes across England, Office for National Statistics (ONS) data shows.

Even high earners in the capital face an uphill battle to afford to buy. The highest 10pc of earners (those with salaries of more than £90,000 a year) will be paying six times their salary, on average, to afford property in London.

To buy in London, first-time buyers now need to save £124,688 on average for a deposit based on a £511,514 average property price, according to data from Halifax. This is far higher than the national average deposit of £61,090 for the average house price of £311,034.

Saving for a house while renting is also increasingly difficult. Average rents in the capital have been rising for years and now stand at £2,121 per month, according to Zoopla.

Figures from the ONS showed that last year, Londoners earning the median salary were spending more than 40pc of their salary on rent, reducing their ability to put money aside.

New-builds are pushed further out of the reach of buyers because they tend to be more expensive than older, equivalent properties.

Newly finished properties in London come with a price premium of £3,300 per square meter when compared with older properties, according to research published earlier this year by property market analysts Investropa.

Hudson says new housing developments in London have all but “disappeared” as a result. The number of new building starts in London has fallen by over 70pc in the past year, according to the Centre for Policy Studies, a think tank.

Historically, developers were geared towards a healthy flow of foreign buyers, says Hudson, but these buyers are also turning away from the capital. “Particularly in inner London, [new properties] have been focused on overseas investors for the past 20 years,” he says.

Data from earlier this year released by Hamptons showed foreign buyers were just 3pc of all house hunters in the first three months of 2025, down from 4pc last year and a peak of 8pc in 2009.

Hudson adds: “Fundamentally it comes down to price and type of homes that are being delivered [...] It’s a product and a price point that is simply not attractive or affordable to the vast majority of London residents.”

There are no quick fixes to address the problem, he concludes. “Over the longer term things like planning reform will work but in the short term things are difficult. We’ve already seen flexibility around the affordable housing provision and it’s looking likely at some point we’ll see a new version of Help to Buy.”

A riskier investment

One of the motivations for buying your own home is the hope that its value will increase, leaving you better off and making it easier for you to move up the ladder when you come to sell.

However, for new-builds this is far from guaranteed. Those who choose to buy a new-build in London have often faced stunted house price growth, in part due to high service charge fees attached to their property.

Average values for all kinds of new-builds, including flats and houses, grew by 3.1pc last year. This was less than half of the house price growth enjoyed by owners of older properties during that time, according to data from Hamptons.

New-build flats had particularly weak price growth at just under 3pc, compared with 5.2pc for older flats.

Buyers of new-builds are also more likely to make a loss when it comes to selling their home in the capital, the same figures reveal.

Among sellers who lost money in London last year, one third were owners of new-build flats. Overall, 9pc of those making a loss sold older properties while 30pc were selling new-builds.

Added to this is the fact that almost all new developments are sold on a leasehold basis and come with a yearly service charge that is supposed to pay for amenities and basic maintenance of shared areas in residential buildings.

But recent research by the Greater London Authority (GLA) found leaseholders were spending on average £3,912 a year, or £326 a month, on service charges. One in 10 surveyed by the GLA were spending more than £7,000 per year, reducing their resell value and in some instances making them un-mortgageable.

The GLA report also highlighted complaints of poor transparency in new shared-ownership schemes, where residents pay rent and a mortgage for the right to resell a fraction of a property, over fees charged for maintenance that is poorly done or left incomplete.

Separate research by Hamptons last year showed that the average service charge for a flat in London was £2,633, up 40pc in five years.

David Fell, an analyst at Hamptons, said these charges were increasingly being avoided by buyers in the city.

“A lot of the new-build schemes [have] lots of amenities and you are paying a service charge for more amenities, but the cost of running those has risen compared with a Victorian house that’s been split into two or three. Buyers don’t want to open themselves up to paying for all of those things,” says Fell.

This has had a marked impact at the top end of London’s housing market, where new luxury housing developments also come with yearly service charges for residents that are often justified by a wide array of on-site leisure facilities and management staff.

Mark Pollack, of London-based estate agents Aston Chase, says buyers are being put off by uncapped monthly charges when purchasing property. “People are more forensic about service charges when they look into a building than they used to be. Developers have seen management alongside a new block of flats as an opportunity to extract further money [and] leaseholders want far more accountability now.”

Red tape is blocking building

Under mounting pressure to resuscitate housebuilding in London, last week Sir Sadiq dropped his affordable housing quota for new developments from 35pc to 20pc in an effort to make the city more attractive to developers.

The London Mayor set a target to build 88,000 new homes in the capital every year. But he also set strict rules for developers to discount properties, initially planning for half of all new homes to be sold below market value.

The policy has been blamed for making building in London difficult and unaffordable, as have new rules for building residential blocks set by the Building Safety Regulator (BSR), a quango established in the aftermath of the Grenfell Tower fire.

The BSR applies some of the strictest rules to residential blocks that are over 18m tall or with seven or more storeys, a form of development that is common in London. These buildings are required to go through a three-stage approvals process during planning and construction.

data.title: Beleaguered auditing giant fined £6.5m for faking documents
data.description:
An embattled UK accountancy firm has been fined £6.5m by Britain’s audit watchdog after one of its former senior managers faked signatures on audit reports.

On Thursday, the Financial Reporting Council (FRC) fined BDO and two of its former partners for failing to spot dishonest conduct carried out by Amanda Nightingale while she was a senior manager at the firm from 2015 to 2019.

Last November Ms Nightingale was banned by the FRC from working as an auditor for 20 years after it was found that she created fake audit evidence and inserted forged electronic signatures into Companies House reports without her colleagues’ knowledge.

The FRC said deficiencies in BDO’s systems had let Ms Nightingale act dishonestly and remain undetected. In addition to a £6.5m fine, the FRC told BDO to repay audit fees charged on the reports.

Jamie Symington, from the FRC, said: “The failings admitted by BDO and the two partners enabled the senior manager’s dishonest course of conduct to go undetected over several years.”

John Everingham and Kevin Cook, who were both audit engagement partners at BDO, were hit with fines worth £189,000 and £90,000 respectively for approving Ms Nightingale’s reports.

Mr Symington added: “Mr Everingham and Mr Cook were responsible as audit engagement partners for the overall quality of the audits they supervised, and they both adopted a fundamentally flawed approach to this role which involved a serious abrogation of their responsibilities.”

The sanctions come as another blow to BDO which has faced criticism from regulators in both Britain and the US over the quality of its audit work.

In July, the FRC said an inspection of BDO’s audit work had shown the firm was performing “significantly short of expectations”, with half of the reports it inspected showing serious shortcomings.

BDO has separately faced scrutiny over its audit of failed US car parts group First Brands, which saw it give the firm the all-clear just months before it filed for bankruptcy.

First Brands’ collapse caused some of the world’s top lenders to suffer hits to their balance sheets, from loans given to the heavily indebted firm.

BDO is currently the world’s fifth largest accountancy firm, behind the “big four”: Deloitte, EY, KPMG and PwC.

A BDO spokesman said: “We have been determined to learn from what happened. As the FRC states, since discovering the senior manager’s misconduct, BDO has ‘worked to remediate and strengthen relevant systems and controls’. We will continue to monitor and, where necessary, improve controls enhancements with the oversight of our regulator.”

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your savings returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

While rates might be on the way down, it’s still possible to find savings accounts paying decent returns.

With so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Claire Jones of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Claire says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Al Rayan Bank 12 month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.35pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,480 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is FirstBank UK’s 2 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 4.11pc AER, which would generate £3,288 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – Trading 212 Instant Access Cash Isa pays 4.53pc AER, generating interest of £906 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose Paragon Bank’s instant access account, paying 4.09pc AER, earning £818 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.1pc to 4.38pc, equivalent to between £4,106 and £4,386 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Claire says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Paragon Bank, which pays 4.09pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,272 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the Gatehouse Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.16pc AER, earning £3,328 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the JN Bank 3 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4pc, earning £800 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.35pc with the Al Rayan Bank 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £870 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-24 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 3.85pc to 4.14pc, or £3,856 to £4,142, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Claire says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.53pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £89 a year.”

Scenario 4

A retired man in his 60s with £100,000 in cash. He doesn’t need access to all of his money immediately, but wants to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Claire says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Paragon Bank, paying 4.09pc AER, earning £1,022 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a 6-month fixed term account from Gatehouse Bank, paying 4.24pc AER, earning £1,050 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Al Rayan Bank, paying 4.35pc, which would generate £1,087 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from FirstBank UK, paying 4.11pc AER, earning £1,038 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.19pc, generating around £4,197 in annual interest, with funds maturing every 6 to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”

data.title: Eurostar orders UK’s first fleet of double-decker trains
data.description:
A major fleet of double-decker trains is to operate in Britain for the first time, but passengers have been warned not to expect twice as many seats.

Eurostar said it will purchase up to 50 two-storey trains from Alstom for use on routes including London St Pancras International to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam via the Channel Tunnel.

The fully electric fleet will be named Eurostar Celestia, which is derived from the Latin word “caelestis”, meaning “heavenly”.

The order will cost Eurostar €2bn (£1.7bn), yet the trains will have only 20pc more seats than the firm’s existing fleet of 17 single-decker e320s from Siemens.

That is because the carriages will be fitted with interior steps either in the middle or at each end, taking a significant chunk out of the additional space.

They will also be barely six inches taller than the current Siemens trains, with the floor lowered to accommodate the extra deck, requiring people to take a step down when boarding.

Virgin Trains, which plans to launch Channel Tunnel services in competition with Eurostar, said this month that it had decided against ordering the same Avelia Horizon model from Alstom because the stairs would have impacted service standards.

Virgin Trains said that the stairs would hamper the ability to serve at-seat meals, which are part of its efforts to offer a more upmarket service than available on Eurostar.

A limited trial of two double-decker SR Class 4DD trains was conducted on services between Dartford in Kent and Charing Cross in central London in the 1950s and 1960s, but they were withdrawn in 1971 because they were considered too cramped and expensive to maintain.

Gwendoline Cazenave, Eurostar’s chief executive, said the trains will nevertheless offer “exceptional comfort” and “new surprises” for passengers.

Eurostar said it is scheduled to begin receiving the first six Celestia models in January 2031, with commercial services launching in May 2031.

The trains, which will run in pairs through the Channel Tunnel – providing around 1,000 seats – will be the first major fleet of double-deckers on the UK’s railways.

Double-decker trains are a common sight on the Continent, but the vast majority of the UK’s rail network is unable to accommodate them because of problems such as bridges not being high enough.

But the high-speed line between St Pancras and the Channel Tunnel was built to European standards, enabling it to be used by higher trains.

Ms Cazenave said the order is key to Eurostar’s “ambitious growth strategy” of reaching 30 million passengers per year, up from 19.5 million in 2024.

Passengers in all classes of travel will be able to choose between a seat on the upper or lower deck, with no price difference. They will enjoy enhancements such as more legroom and additional areas for bikes and wheelchair users.

Eurostar confirmed an initial order for 30 trains with an option for a further 20. They will operate in addition to the e320s, bringing the total fleet up to 67 trains and enabling more frequent services on existing routes and the addition of new destinations, including Frankfurt and Geneva.

Richard Bowker, a head of National Express and the Strategic Rail Authority, said that while double-decker trains will add some capacity they will also mean longer boarding times at stations, potentially extending journeys.

He said that could be bad news for campaigners seeking the restoration of Eurostar services to Ashford and Ebbsfleet in Kent.

Growing competition

It comes just a week before the Office of Rail and Road (ORR), the industry regulator, is due to choose between three rival attempts to provide competition to Eurostar for the first time in its 30-year history.

Sir Richard Branson’s Virgin has agreed a deal for 12 Avelia Stream trains from Alstom, the successor to the Pendolino tilting trains it once operated in the UK.

Italy’s state-owned FS Italiane Group plans to operate the same Frecciarossa 1000 trains it runs between Milan and Paris. Meanwhile, Gemini Trains, chaired by Labour peer Lord Berkeley, would purchase single-decker trains from Siemens.

Eurostar will be forced to spend €80m to expand the only UK maintenance facility for Channel Tunnel trains. The remaining space at the Temple Mills site in London is to be handed over to the new entrant chosen by the ORR.

Eurostar is majority-owned by the French state railway company SNCF Connect.

The UK sold its stake in the operator to private companies for £757m in 2015.

data.title: Is this really the time for segregated trains?
data.description:
With Islamic extremism on the rise in the UK - is now really the time to be segregating the sexes on trains?

There were rightly howls of outrage when women were banned from taking part in a fun run across Tower Hamlets earlier this month.

The 5km Muslim Charity Run in Victoria Park, East London, was billed as “inclusive” and “family-friendly” even though organisers at the East London Mosque forbade any female over 12 from joining in.

This seemingly obvious act of sexual discrimination is currently being investigated under the Equalities Act.

Now there’s a call for women-only carriages on trains that would surely play into the hands of those who advocate keeping men and women apart. 

A petition is gaining momentum after 21-year-old student activist Camille Brown called for at least one women-only carriage on all Tube trains after experiencing sexual harassment on the London Underground.

While it’s true that reported sexual offences have risen year on year by more than 10 per cent on Tubes, trains and buses across the capital, this isn’t the way to deal with it.

If women don’t feel safe on public transport then the solution is to criminalise the offenders – not segregate their victims. 

This would do nothing to tackle the root cause of harassment and misogynistic behaviour but instead shift the responsibility from perpetrators to the women they prey on. It also runs the risk of emboldening perverts by forcing women into designated “safe zones”. 

Countries like Japan and India have women-only carriages but reports suggest harassment hasn’t disappeared, it’s just been displaced.

True equality surely means women (and men) being able to move freely and safely in public? 

We all want to protect single sex spaces, but we shouldn’t be actively turning unisex public spaces into men-free zones. It’s not just defeatist but downright discriminatory.

data.title: Hedge funds are piling into nuclear fuel – and you could make money too
data.description:
Among the blizzard of announcements issued by the Trump administration each day, you might be forgiven for not noticing an addition to the list of critical minerals deemed essential to the US economy. Earlier this month, the department of energy added a newcomer to the list: uranium.

A few days later, the news wires carried a story about Trump’s sons investing in a small US-listed firm that enriches uranium for nuclear power plants. More generally, the Trump administration is looking to revitalise the nuclear sector – a renaissance I’ve already written about – through deals such as the strategic partnership, worth at least $80bn (£61bn), between power unit developer Westinghouse and Brookfield Asset Management to “accelerate the deployment of nuclear power”.

It is part of a broader emerging story that is getting hedge funds excited. Put simply, if the world is to increase its use of nuclear power, it’ll need more uranium oxide, also known as yellowcake, and it will also need to enrich it at scale without involving Russia.

What has piqued the interest of hedge funds is the suspicion that this rush to nuclear will outpace efforts to supply this nuclear fuel at scale and squeeze the spot price of uranium, making it even more valuable than it already is. This would help to boost the share prices of several UK-listed funds.

To understand what’s happening, let’s take a step back and explain the dynamics of the uranium market.

Nuclear power station owners require enriched uranium oxide to fuel their reactors. This input isn’t a significant portion of their total costs and, by and large, energy companies have been happy to buy yellowcake from major producers such as Cameco and Kazatomprom in the former Soviet Union on long-term contracts. As a result, they have avoided the spot markets, where goods can be bought for immediate delivery, favoured by hedge funds and financial institutions.

But an upset in the balance of demand and supply might be looming.

On the demand side, a recent World Nuclear Fuel Report 2025 projected a sharp rise in uranium demand from current levels of 68,920 tonnes of uranium (tU) each year to 150,525 by 2040. Why? Because all those new nuclear power stations planned around the world will need extra fuel.

The snag is that supply isn’t keeping up. Both major producers have recently cut back their output projections. Cameco has reduced production guidance for its large Canadian mines by 22pc, while Kazatomprom has announced a 10pc reduction in output, likely prioritising Chinese buyers in the future.

Running a big uranium mine is an expensive, permit-heavy, long-term business. Developing new pits takes time and a lot of money. Although supply will probably adjust over a few decades, there may well be a lag. That means we could see a sudden surge in demand for uranium on the spot market as power stations seek out supply.

We’ve already seen sudden surges in the spot price of yellowcake over the last few years. That could happen again, especially as financial firms such as hedge funds wade into the market and snap up reserves for themselves.

Anticipating this, two big players – a Canadian firm called Sprott, and a UK Aim-listed investment company called Yellowcake – have raised extra capital for their uranium reserve funds, and used it to buy the fuel.

For the record, I own shares in Yellowcake. It’s popular with many UK investors and its share price trades at a near 10pc discount to the spot value of the yellowcake uranium reserves it holds in Canada. Geiger Counter, another UK-listed fund, may also benefit as spot prices rise. So too could a long tail of uranium ETFs, including the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF here in the UK, which would pump more money into uranium mining companies and their equity.

However, before we get too carried away with the hedge funds’ bullish arguments, it’s worth noting that the uranium price, currently around $78/lb, is significantly lower than its peak above $100 in 2024. Trading volumes on the spot market have also been low so far this year.

Nevertheless, even the current level represents a significant improvement over the sub-$30 prices seen in the late 2000s. And speak to those close to this sector, such as investment house Ocean Wall, and you will find that they expect energy producers and utilities to re-enter the market by year-end as the major investment firms continue to snap up spare stock on the spot markets. Inventories might be running short.

Lurking beyond the debate about spot market prices is something even more interesting: the processing and enrichment of uranium. In simple terms, for nuclear power, you need to take mined uranium, convert it into a concentrated form called yellowcake or uranium oxide and then undergo more conversion and enrichment to use as fuel.

The snag here, much remarked upon by security hawks, is that Russia is the dominant player in enriched uranium. The United States currently imports more than 99pc of its uranium, whether enriched or not, and relies heavily on foreign suppliers, including Russia. To make matters worse, there’s an impending ban on Russian uranium imports set to take effect in the next few years.

There is one bit of good news for UK readers – the UK has its own enrichment facilities and is not at all reliant on Russia. But that’s not true for much of the rest of Europe. Someone, somewhere, needs to fill the gap.

Big players like Cameco are active in yellowcake processing, and they are also investing in next-generation enrichment businesses. However, investors are increasingly scouring the world for new players in this space, and two names keep emerging, both listed on international markets: ASP Isotopes on the Nasdaq; and Silex Systems on the Australian stock market. If the nuclear renaissance does gather momentum, demand for new names connected to the nuclear power value chain to invest in will intensify.
