data.title: The BBC may hate him, but it is making the world safe for Trump
data.description:
Looking back, they might have been on to something. Supporters of Robert Mugabe, the late dictator of Zimbabwe, were not always hailed for their prescience, but at one rally long ago their banners read: “Down with the naked lies of BBC.”

As I stood in that crowd in April 2000, my sympathies were firmly with the BBC, partly because the tyrant’s followers kept jostling and shoving me just in case I worked for that organisation.

But the main reason was that everyone knew how Mugabe’s problem with the BBC was not that it told “naked lies”, but rather the fully-clothed truth about his calamitous rule. Even worse, he was tormented by the thought that many of his own people trusted the BBC far more than they trusted him.

Like tyrants from Vladimir Putin to the “Supreme Leader” of Iran, Mugabe hated the BBC for its virtues not its vices.

And that, for me, was the BBC’s claim to greatness: it used to be loathed by all the right people. Dictators were incensed by the mere existence of a news service broadcasting from London that their populations trusted and their regimes could not control.

So the real tragedy of recent events is how the BBC has turned itself into an enemy not of a despot but of the freely elected president of the world’s second-biggest democracy. And whereas the old autocrats would complain about what the BBC was doing right – exposing their abuses – Donald Trump’s grievance is fully justified.

Even the BBC has finally admitted that Panorama was obviously wrong to have edited his speech from January 6 2021 in a wholly misleading way.

Heaven knows there is a sordid underside to Trump’s rule. Look at the damage done to American institutions or the conflicts of interest between the president’s private business empire and his public office. Look at the purging of US intelligence agencies of anyone who dared assess that Russia interfered in Trump’s favour in the 2016 election.

If the BBC had infuriated Trump by exposing his genuine excesses, then fair-minded people would know where to stand. Instead, the BBC chose to discredit itself by misrepresenting Trump and then failing to own up until Michael Prescott’s explosive memo was published by this newspaper.

“It’s difficult to be overly impressed by how we are operating,” a former BBC foreign correspondent tells me, with calculated understatement. “A lot of mistakes have not been acted upon or even swept under the carpet.”

That leads to perhaps the biggest tragedy of all. By its cavalier treatment of Trump, the BBC has given legitimacy to his argument that a malevolent media will do whatever it can to engulf him in a tidal wave of “fake news” designed to sweep him from office.

Trump responds to any criticism or exposure with that convenient claim and it should be the mission of every journalist to demonstrate its emptiness. Instead the BBC has made Trump’s stock reaction far easier and even endowed it with more than a shred of credibility.

Rather than shining a light on his excesses – the classic function of a free media – the BBC has made it easier for Trump to get away with them.

Thanks to social media, the world is already awash with fakery and invention. That phenomenon will only get worse as AI deepfakes are perfected and it becomes ever harder to distinguish between fantasy and reality, to spot the difference between an artificially created clip of a politician saying something outrageous and the genuine article.

The BBC’s great opportunity was to stand as the world’s strongest bulwark against this danger. By fulfilling its stated mission to provide “accurate and impartial news, current affairs and factual programming”, the BBC could have secured its place as an indispensable British public service with a global role.

This year, the BBC’s international audience came to a weekly total of 418 million, including 313 million reached on the World Service. BBC Persian’s audience rose by 38 per cent to 24 million, despite being banned – and often jammed – by the Iranian regime. Every week the BBC reaches one in every four people in Iran.

At this point, it would be customary to say that these numbers show how the BBC is a colossal national asset. But it will only keep those audiences – and be worthy of their trust – if it really does serve as the foremost line of defence against fakery and manipulation.

I struggle to see how it can achieve this if some BBC journalists find it hard to see anything wrong with manipulating the words of a national leader and misleading their audience.

The overall impression of the Prescott memo is of an organisation so blinkered by a uniform ideology that it cannot be trusted on subjects as important as Donald Trump, the Arab-Israeli conflict, British history and anything to do with race.

The new leadership of the BBC will have to regard this crisis as a last opportunity for real change to rebuild that trust. Their mission should be to ensure that all future complaints, like those of Robert Mugabe, come from dictators objecting to what the broadcaster does right.

data.title: Tories pledge to ban diversity targets for City firms
data.description:
Diversity targets imposed on City boardrooms would be axed under a new policy unveiled by the Conservatives to slash red tape.

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, announced proposals on Thursday to ban Britain’s main financial watchdogs from introducing new rules on diversity and repeal existing regulations imposed on major high street banks and insurers.

Under the Tory plans, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) would be forced to focus strictly on financial issues when making new rules by requiring the regulators to obtain ministerial approval for any “non-financial” rules.

Sir Mel said the party would also sweep away any rules for banks and insurers which do not relate to financial stability, competition, consumer protection or growth, it it wins power at the next election.

The move is designed to free City firms from having to comply with diversity and inclusion rules, which have increased compliance costs for businesses.

These include FCA diversity targets for banks and investment managers to have 40pc of board seats assigned to women and at least one seat occupied by a person from an ethnic minority background.

“If we want to compete in the world, British business must be freed from unnecessary costs and red tape,” Sir Mel said. “For too long, governments have imposed burdens on firms around issues such as climate change and diversity, while encouraging the independent regulators to do the same.”

The proposals come amid a wider shake-up at the regulators following pressure from Rachel Reeves to cut red tape.

Earlier this year, the Chancellor urged Nikhil Rathi, the FCA chief, and Sam Woods, the PRA boss, to come up with ideas to boost UK growth after claiming City regulations were acting like a “boot on the neck” of business.

The move prompted a major rethink from regulators, with Mr Rathi pledging to roll back some of the more onerous rules. However, he has warned that greater risk-taking will lead to a greater chance of consumer harm in future.

It came after the FCA and the PRA scrapped plans to introduce new diversity and inclusion reporting rules for City firms over concerns about imposing extra costs on banks.

The watchdogs had faced a backlash over the plans from City executives, before abandoning the proposals in March following Donald Trump’s crackdown on diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives in the US.

The Tory proposals are part of a blitz by the party on business regulations, which included an announcement last week to scrap mandatory reporting of sustainability and climate change issues.

Sir Mel added: “I want the UK to have the most dynamic financial services industry in the world. That means companies should be allowed to focus on delivering value for customers and shareholders, not on targets, quotas and reporting requirements dictated by government.”

The FCA and PRA were contacted for comment.

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: To restore Britain, start with the rail dining car
data.description:
Among stiff competition, few experiences more totally obliterate any lingering faith in Britain today than the average train journey. Penned like cattle into stiff plastic seats, stifled by delays and cancellations, bombarded by youths playing music out loud and preyed upon by conductors yearning to triple charge you for what you thought was the right ticket, the average railway journey is an express train into misery.

Yet amid this litany of woe, there is the worst offender. I’ve had some depressing meals in my time, from school dinner slop to peculiar post-club kebabs. But no culinary catastrophe comes close to the miserable pickings offered by your average rail buffet car. Remembering that half of what is mislabelled a menu is missing at any one time, one is usually offered a bleak selection of stale sandwiches, tepid bacon rolls, unimaginative crisps and depressing paninis. Before boarding a long-distance train today, I always make a packed lunch.

I wasn’t always so dour. I was a train-mad tot, raised between Thomas the Tank Engine, toy tracks and weekend Tube trips. As I grew older, I wept over pictures of disused stations, put railway posters up with my Margaret Thatcher and Jenna Coleman portraits, and re-watched John Betjeman’s Metro-Land with a fanaticism that would put many a jihadi to shame. But the fantasy has died as my professional obligations have made train travel a daily dread.

It shouldn’t have to be like this. We did once know how to make the experience enjoyable. Travelling by rail through Britain should be joyous. Even if one has to stop off at a crime against architecture grotesquely mislabelled as a station, one yearns to see our country’s magnificence while hurtling between destinations in far greater comfort than a motorway slog. An obvious ground zero for rail restorationism is the dining car.

Anyone who has seen North by Northwest or From Russia with Love can attest to the inherent charm of an old-fashioned dining car. Who wouldn’t want a long-distance railway journey enhanced by tucking in to a three-course meal with a decent claret or three? Who wouldn’t rather enjoy a cocktail as the country passes by than a lukewarm lager or oddly frosty coffee? Luxury train journeys with dining experiences – like the Northern Belle, branded “Britain’s poshest train”, and available for booking for Christmas dinner – are doing great business. Why not bring this back to the masses?

There is a 150-odd year tradition of doing so. The Pullman dining car is synonymous with luxury. Originally built by the Pullman Company for dining, sleeping and lounge use, the term still applies to a few remaining high-end train dining services. But the number of them has shrunk precipitously. When the railways were privatised in 1995 almost 250 trains a day offered a dining car. Within two decades, that had shrunk to only four. Privatisation saw passenger numbers double and investment increase nine-old, but at the cost of making journeys soulless.

With Labour’s renationalisation push, there is a chance for a reset. Yet the first returns of renewed state-control are predictably unpromising, with delays and cancellations on South Western Railway rising by 50 per cent since nationalisation. Labour’s technocrats lack the vision required to restore our railways. Which is why the cause of the dining car should be taken up by a politician with a far better appreciation of a long lunch’s merits: Nigel Farage.

The Reform leader may prefer private helicopter to rail. But with his newfound penchant for nationalisation, Farage is already fishing in this pool. What could be more appropriate than pledging to put a Boisdale’s on every inter-city service? Back in 2010, Ukip pledged to “make the Circle line a circle again”. That is the spirit in which Farage shall save our trains.

William Atkinson is assistant content editor at The Spectator

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Heathrow boss buys ‘extra-loud phone’ after sleeping through airport blackout
data.description:
The boss of Heathrow bought himself an “extra-loud phone” after sleeping through multiple calls when a power outage led to the closure of the airport.

Thomas Woldbye was asleep when his senior staff decided to suspend all flights because of a fire at Hayes electrical substation, which supplies about a third of Heathrow’s electricity needs.

The blaze started at Europe’s busiest airport late on the evening of March 20 and flights were suspended until about 6pm the next day.

A Heathrow-commissioned inquiry led by Ruth Kelly, the former transport secretary, found Mr Woldbye was not woken by alerts sent to his mobile, which was on a bedside table, because it had gone into silent mode “without him being aware”.

Asked at the Airlines 2025 conference in Westminster on Monday if he had since obtained an “extra-loud phone”, Mr Woldbye replied: “Oh, absolutely. And more.”

‘Nothing should depend on me alone’

He said he was “personally devastated” by his phone being on silent, describing it as a “technical glitch”.

But he added that “nothing in this world should be depending on me alone” and “all the right people were there to take the right decisions”.

More than 270,000 passengers’ journeys were disrupted by the incident.

Mr Woldbye, who was paid £3.2m last year, said businesses “need to be able to trust our utilities” and we are “not in a country where everybody needs a back-up”.

He added: “The Neso [National Energy System Operator] report made it very, very clear there were major failures on the part of National Grid and they should have been fixed.

“We should not have been in that situation in the first place.”

The Heathrow boss was also asked whether a similar power outage today would close the airport down again.

He replied: “It would, probably, for about six hours.”

Mr Woldbye added that “lots of procedures” had changed since March to ensure that recovering from another sudden power loss would be “faster”.

One plan included installing “more backup generators”, following criticisms that the airport had replaced diesel-fired equipment with a net zero-compliant biomass plant, which needs mains electricity for it to be started up.

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: Eurostar needs more competition than just Branson’s Virgin
data.description:
There will be some cabin crew in bright red uniforms. There will probably be some loud music and plenty of dry ice. And Sir Richard Branson could almost certainly show up in a rocket, a balloon or perhaps even a submarine, pulling some kind of stunt or other.

We can expect plenty of glitzy razzamatazz when the first Virgin train heads out of London to the Continent in a few years’ time, breaking Eurostar’s monopoly of rail travel through the Channel Tunnel.

And yet we should not stop there. There should be lots of operators competing on that route – not just two.

After a long struggle, Virgin last week finally won its battle to run trains to the rest of Europe after securing approval from the Office of Rail and Road.

It will still need to get safety approvals from regulators in both the UK and the European Union, negotiate platform space and work out a deal with Eurostar. But it hopes to have a service to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam up and running by the end of the decade.

For the first time in the three decades since the tunnel linking the UK to the rest of Europe was opened, passengers will finally be able to choose which company they want to travel with.

And at least one rail route in the UK will get some much-needed competition, even while the rest of the network is steadily nationalised.

It is about time. Eurostar has, just like any monopoly, become complacent and expensive.

When I checked on Friday afternoon, if I wanted to book a trip to Paris for the following weekend, the cheapest return train ticket would cost me £260 return, and well over £300 if I decided to travel at a civilised hour, while I could get a return easyJet flight for £110.

The prices are way too high, especially given that most of the infrastructure is now three decades old. It is not as if the service is amazing either, even if it recently unveiled spiffy new gender neutral uniforms for the staff.

On Trustpilot, the train service has an average review ranking of just 2.1 out of a possible 5.0, which suggests, to put it politely, that there is a little room for improvement.

That is not nearly good enough. The entire point of the tunnel was to replace flights with train routes, both to reduce congestion at the airports and to cut carbon emissions. The train service should have driven the budget airlines out of the market on that route by now.

Instead, eye-watering ticket prices mean many of us prefer to get a cheap flight instead. As for the plans for seamless train journeys that would link into the Continent’s increasingly sophisticated high-speed rail network, and whisk us all effortlessly around France, and on to Germany, Italy or Spain, they remain on the drawing board, and have done for decades.

It is a huge missed opportunity, and one that will only start to be fixed once the tunnel is finally opened up to competition.

Here is the problem, however. Why only allow Virgin to offer an alternative service? There were three more bidders for the route who were turned down.

As well as Virgin, Gemini Trains, chaired by the Labour peer Lord Berkeley, as well as Trenalitia, the main operating arm of the Italian rail operator and the Spanish start-up Evolyn, were all interested in bidding for the route.

Instead of just one new operator, we could have had four or five. Sure, there are issues with hangar space. But even in Sir Sadiq Khan’s London, where virtually nothing gets built any more, it is surely not impossible to build some extra storage capacity for the trains if that is what is needed, and if some extra platform space is needed at St Pancras, then surely that would be possible as well.

In reality, a whole series of competitors would genuinely kickstart the market. We need a range of different services: a British Airways-style business class offering, for example, for all the people travelling to Brussels or Paris on expenses, alongside a much cheaper Ryanair-style service for people who are getting away for the weekend and would rather spend their money on dinner out than the train fare.

Sure, not all of us want to make a seven or eight-hour rail trip to Milan, or take a 10-hour through-train to Madrid, but a few people will, partly because they want to cut their carbon footprint, or perhaps because they just happen, perfectly understandably, to prefer the old-fashioned romance of the train to the cattle-car conditions of most airports.

We could even see direct routes from other British cities, perhaps with trains running from Manchester straight through to Paris, or else from Birmingham direct to Brussels.

The important point is this: we can’t know for certain which routes would be profitable or what the levels of demand might be.

But we can be sure of one thing: if we allow lots of different operators access to the rail tracks running under the English Channel, then plenty of different possible routes will be tried out, and we will find out very quickly which ones work and which ones don’t.

A duopoly is a lot better than a monopoly, but only just. It can very quickly slip back into a cosy cartel, with both companies quietly agreeing to keep prices high and to keep the competition limited. It is less hassle and more profitable.

In reality, the tunnel has been a squandered asset. It should be opened up to a whole range of different services, and it will be far better off from a blast of fierce competition between competitors.

data.title: Why Jane wouldn’t mind Austen Country being concreted over
data.description:
It is a truth universally acknowledged, that any bucolic English village in possession of a good few free fields, must be in want of a housing development. Not quite, say the villagers of east Hampshire, who are arguing that a proposed development of 12,000 new homes between Farnham and Alresford will leave the historic countryside which inspired Jane Austen “concreted over”. “Save Jane Austen Country”, the cry goes.

There’s every reason to be sympathetic to those opposed to the plans, which are still in the early development stage known as a “land availability assessment”. If the building did go ahead, it would likely decimate wildlife in Hampshire’s chalk streams and destroy local farmland, and there has been no indication it would be supported by a corresponding expansion of local services like GPs and schools.

But when it comes to the claims that this development would see “Austen Country” destroyed, I come over as disagreeable as Lady Catherine de Bourgh. Bar the scenes of Bath’s creamy crescents and the drama of Emma’s excursion to Box Hill in Surrey, is Jane Austen really a landscape writer?

Before anyone cites Colin Firth’s infamous emergence from the lake, it’s worth remembering that the sultry scene was a decidedly Nineties addition to the television adaptation of Pride and Prejudice, rather than one dreamt up by Miss Austen herself. And, besides, you would have a hard time proving it is Hampshire’s rugged beauty that any viewer’s mind is focused on in that exact moment.

Jane Austen is a writer focused on interior scenes: awkward tea parties, tense balls and the difficult politics of a country house stay. Unlike the wuthering moors of the Brontës’ novels, the emotional heart of Austen’s novels is always in the dim light of a drawing room or rickety carriage, or contained within the handwritten lines of a letter.

Even if her heroines do get themselves into scrapes in their surrounding landscapes – a sprained ankle and rain-induced fever jolt the plot along in Sense and Sensibility – you would be hard-pressed to argue that Austen is a nature novelist, more inspired by the sparkling dew on the grass than by glint in the eye of a locally-stationed young army officer.

The “Save Austen Country” campaign relies on seeing “Austen Country” as some perfect, historical Eden; an apolitical arcadia, which can’t risk being touched or sullied by modern life. It’s a romantic fallacy, but one which wouldn’t have held up even in Austen’s time. Her landscapes hum to the background of Regency-era politics, with dashing soldiers in military encampments and, in the case of Mansfield Park, a suggestion that some of splendid wealth on display is derived from colonial trade. Indeed, Austen sends up one of her characters – the dreamy, novel-obsessed Catherine Morland in Northanger Abbey – for wishing for a landscape of gothic “ancient oaks”, and being disappointed by the ease of “modern taste” she finds instead.

Austen may well have been inspired by the Hampshire countryside – as those in the campaign point out, it is certainly a beautiful place to live and write – but there is something disingenuous about using her work to oppose new houses. This was a woman who came from a middle-class family; a family who were continually squeezed for money. Perhaps the 21st-century Jane Austen will write from Hampshire too, but from a new build instead.

data.title: I had a front row seat for the BBC’s last major crisis. They’ve learnt nothing
data.description:
I notice that Katie Razzall, the BBC’s media editor, has been defending her use of the word “unprecedented’’ for the current mess the BBC finds itself in.

For many, like myself, who worked for the corporation more than 20 years ago, it feels like history repeating itself and a critical lesson from two decades ago forgotten.

In 2004, the corporation lost Greg Dyke, its director general, and Gavyn Davies, its chairman. At the time, it was the biggest crisis the BBC had ever faced.

Back then, as now, under huge political pressure, senior management failed to quickly apologise for a serious editorial failing. Instead, Dyke and the organisation adopted a bunker mentality and went all out to defend its journalism.

The crisis had begun a year earlier in the run-up to the Iraq war. The corporation had broadcast reports that alleged the Labour government had “sexed-up” a security dossier to help justify a very unpopular war.

The Government immediately attacked the BBC’s journalism.

BBC management stood by the reports and said they had a credible source. The source was scientist Dr David Kelly, who tragically died by suicide when his name was exposed in the press. His death led to a public inquiry led by Lord Hutton.

The inquiry’s findings were damning. Hutton found the BBC’s editorial and management processes were “defective”. Davies resigned immediately and Dyke followed (or was pushed) days later.

I worked for the BBC at the time and it felt cataclysmic – our journalism had been undermined and it was a huge loss to see Dyke, who was much-loved, being forced to resign.

So Razzall’s assertion that the current mess is unprecedented doesn’t quite work for me. Some of Hutton’s conclusions on BBC management could be applied to the current crisis, as could the mindset of the organisation in the past week.

The corporation, under attack after recent scandals, appears again to have gone into bunker mentality. It got lost in a narrative that the Right-wing press and politically motivated board members were out to get it.

So, when a serious editorial breach was exposed, it initially defended it as “normal practice” and has focused on its detractors. It failed to see that it needed to act quickly and apologise. Management fell into the same trap it did 20 years ago.

The rest is now itself history.

Mr Davie joined the BBC in 2005. He would have come into a BBC still scarred by the Hutton Inquiry. And yet here we are again.

Whoever takes the helm now needs to keep a clear head when mistakes are made and learn from the past.

Tamsin O’Brien is a journalist and former BBC senior manager. She currently owns a news website, The Stray Ferret, in North Yorkshire.

data.title: Revealed: the best savings accounts on the market right now
data.description:
Regardless of why you’re saving – for a house, a holiday, your children’s future or as a buffer to cover emergencies – you’ll want to make sure your money is working as hard as possible.

To help you get the most out of your savings, Telegraph Money has launched a range of “best buy” tables showing the highest rates available across a variety of savings accounts. These tables update automatically every day, with data provided by Savings Data Limited.

It’s more important than ever to make sure your savings are receiving a competitive rate. The Bank of England has cut rates to 4pc, triggering many savings providers to reduce their own rates. Given the latest inflation figure for September measured 3.8pc, it will become increasingly likely that the value of your money will be eroded by rising prices.

Our tables include the best cash Isa rates – whether you want an easy-access, notice, fixed-rate or variable rate account – and we also list the best Lifetime Isas if you’re saving for a house or your retirement.

If you have already maxed out your Isa, or want to save outside of the tax-free wrapper, we track the best easy-access accounts and interest-paying current accounts.

Those who prefer to save monthly should refer to the table of top-paying regular savings accounts, while savers happy to lock their money up for longer in pursuit of higher returns could benefit from our lists of the best fixed-rate bonds and notice accounts.

We also track the best Junior cash Isas and the best children’s savings accounts for parents putting money away for their family’s futures.

In this article, we will cover:

  • The best fixed-rate bonds
  • The best variable savings account rates
  • The best current account rates
  • The best Isa rates
  • The best children’s accounts
  • How to choose the right account for you

Why do rates matter?

Seeking out an account with a more competitive interest rate can make a huge difference.

For example, take a savings account paying a relatively low 0.75pc. A saver with £50,000 in this account will earn around £375 a year in interest. Moving the deposit to an account with a better rate can mean a difference of thousands. Take an account at the higher end offering 4.75pc – you’d yield £2,427 a year, an extra £2,052.

Cash loses value over time if it is left in an account that pays less than the inflation rate. At the current inflation rate of 3.8pc, a pot of £1,000 left in a zero-interest account would be worth just £963.39 after a year in real terms, and just £829.88 after five years. Our inflation calculator can show how your savings are affected.

What is a savings account?

Unlike a current account, which you’ll use to pay your bills and for everyday spending, a savings account is designed to be the place where you can stash your spare money away, where it will hopefully grow over time.

There are several different types of savings accounts – some offer variable rates, which can change at any time, while some require you to commit to locking your cash away for a fixed period.

In theory, the more inconvenient an account is, the more interest you should earn – but it’s not always the case.

The best fixed-rate bonds for 2025

Fixed-rate bonds tend to pay the highest rates on the market. Savers need to weigh up whether to take a fixed deal in order to get guaranteed interest for a longer term, or take a chance on a variable rate that could rise over the length of the term.

The best variable savings account rates for 2025

Variable-rate accounts tend to come with more flexibility than fixed-rate deals; often, you can make as many withdrawals as you like without taking a hit on interest, but some specific deals have their own restrictions.

In general, easy-access accounts let you take out money whenever you like; regular saver accounts require you to make deposits regularly; while notice accounts allow you to make withdrawals, but only if you give your provider a prescribed amount of notice.

By clicking through to each of our guides you can find out today’s best rates.

Easy-access savings account

Regular savings account

Notice savings account

The best current account rates for 2025

In some cases, current accounts will pay interest on the balance of money they hold, which can be useful if you don’t want the hassle of transferring money between multiple accounts.

The best Isa rates for 2025

Cash Isas work in a similar way to savings accounts, except that all interest you earn is tax-free – and you’re restricted to depositing up to £20,000 in each tax year. Income tax is payable on savings interest earned outside of an Isa, above an allowance of £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 in the higher-rate band.

Lifetime Isa

Savers who are under 40 and putting money away to buy a home or fund their retirement can open a Lifetime Isa. As well as the tax-free savings growth, the Government rewards savers with a bonus of 25pc, up to £1,000 a year.

The best children’s accounts

If you’re under the age of 18, or saving on behalf of a youngster, you can open a children’s account. Many account providers will let children add and withdraw money from the age of seven, so it can be a good way to teach financial skills early in life.

Children also get their own Isa allowance of £9,000 a year, which means an adult saving on behalf of a child in a Junior Isa can put this extra amount away on top of their own £20,000 allowance.

What is happening to savings rates?

Hundreds of savings accounts currently offer a rate that beats CPI inflation, which measured 3.8pc in September, the same as it was in August, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics.

Savings have broadly been on a downward trajectory since they peaked in summer 2023 – but some rates have been falling more quickly this year, since the Bank of England has made several Bank Rate cuts.

The most competitive rates can be quick to disappear – so if you spot an account that looks good, you may need to act fast.

Alice Haine, of online investment platform Bestinvest by Evelyn Partners, says: “For now, the best savings rates are continuing to outstrip inflation, giving savers who hunted out the top deals a healthy real return on their nest eggs. Locking in a top rate now before the best deals disappear could be a sensible strategy for those with cash languishing in an account delivering dismal returns to ensure their money is working as hard as possible.”

Use our calculator to see how much your savings account is earning you – and how much more interest you could get if you switched.

What is the difference between an Isa, bond, savings and a current account?

A current account is a transactional account that typically pays no interest but gives you a lot of flexibility in how often you access your money.

With a savings account, the bank pays you interest for keeping your money and therefore imposes some restrictions on how many withdrawals you can make.

A fixed-rate bond is a savings account with a fixed term, usually between one and five years. Until the duration of the bond is up, you cannot withdraw your funds, but in exchange for the commitment you will typically benefit from a higher rate.

The difference between an Isa and other types of savings account is that there is no tax charged on the interest. Everyone can save up to £20,000 a year tax-free in an Isa, or £9,000 for children.

How to choose the right account for you

The first thing to consider is whether you might need access to the funds in an emergency. A current account or an easy-access savings account will give you this flexibility.

However, you will get a higher rate if you are willing to lock away your funds for a set period (for example, in a bond). Generally, the longer the period, the higher the rate – however, this is not the case at the moment. Fixed-rate accounts with one and two-year terms are far higher than those with five-year rates.

The other thing to consider is whether you are at risk of exceeding your personal savings allowance. This is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. If you earn more than this in interest outside of an Isa, then you will have to pay income tax.

Use our savings tax interest calculator to work out whether you could breach your allowance, and if you should get an Isa.

data.title: M&S under fire for selling two slices of plain bread for £4.50
data.description:
Marks & Spencer has been criticised for selling two slices of plain bread for as much as £4.50.

The retailer is facing calls to justify the price of its “Pan de Cristal” product, a traditional Spanish bread from the Catalan region.

M&S sells the bread in packs of two slices for as much as £4.50 in some stores. M&S describes the product, also known as glass bread, as “a light, crisp bread perfect for toasting and topping with our crushed tomato bread topper”.

The recommended accompaniment – the 250ml crushed tomato bread topper, a puree made up of tomatoes, olive oil and salt – costs an additional £2.65.

In a recent post on LinkedIn, Stuart Machin, the M&S chief executive, said the product was one of his favourite new releases.

However, customers have raised questions about the product’s value for money. In a video on TikTok, a shopper asked: “What in the hell is this £4 half piece of baguette about?”

Another social media user said: “The joke is, you get this in Spain for €2 [£1.80].”

Chris Young, from the Real Bread Campaign, said M&S should justify the price “given the economies of the enormous scale at which even supposedly ‘artisan’ supermarket loaves are manufactured”.

He said the price point was in the “ballpark that small independents need to charge in order to remain financially viable”, adding: “Can the same be said of a supermarket?”

Pan de Cristal is priced well above M&S’s standard range, costing more than 25 times as much as a white loaf when measured on a weight basis. Customers can buy a thick sliced loaf for 9p per 100g, compared to £2.50 per 100g for the Pan de Cristal.

The Spanish bread even costs more than Sicilian Nocellara Del Belice olive oil and an M&S jar of cherries in kirsch when measured by weight.

The steep prices comes as Britain faces renewed cost of living pressures. The latest figures show that grocery prices rose by 4.7pc in the four weeks to Nov 2 compared with the same period a year earlier.

The Treasury has said tackling food inflation is a “priority”. In a speech this month, Rachel Reeves promised she would “bear down” on inflation in her Budget.

Supermarkets have blamed the Chancellor for fuelling food inflation after raising employers’ National Insurance and minimum wage in last year’s Budget.

Mr Machin last week said shoppers were preparing for the worst in the upcoming Budget later this month. He said: “Customers are worried about rising costs, they’re very worried about tax rises. They think things are heading in the wrong direction.”

A spokesman for M&S said the retailer offered “a broad range of products at different price points” including 75p loaves and half-baguettes at 80p.

They said its Pan de Cristal range was designed to be enjoyed as “a starter or as part of a tapas style meal”, adding that products had proven very popular, with 75,000 packs being sold since launch and more than 5,000 bought per week in summer.

The spokesman said: “We know our customers want different products for different occasions from a weekday breakfast with the family to serving friends restaurant-quality tapas at home using the best authentic Spanish products.

“In fact, we know that more people are choosing to eat something different or special at home instead of going out. Our tapas-style Spanish Pan de Cristal is made with restaurant-quality ingredients including Spanish-made bread from the Catalan region and extra virgin olive oil.”

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: How long between exchange of contracts and completion?
data.description:
It doesn’t matter whether you’re buying your first home or your last, the process can be fraught with tension; you’ll be eager to progress to exchange of contracts and agree a completion date as quickly as you can.

With that in mind, this Telegraph Money guide explains how long you’ll likely need to wait between exchange and completion, including whether it’s possible to get it done and dusted in one day.

  • What does exchange of contracts mean?
  • How long between exchange of contracts and completion date?
  • What happens on completion day?
  • What is the best day to complete a house sale?
  • Exchange and completion FAQs

What does exchange of contracts mean?

The so-called exchange of contracts occurs when both the buyer and the seller have signed and swapped the contracts agreeing the terms of the sale.

By this point the stressful part should (hopefully) be behind you: the sale is now legally binding and neither of you can pull out without taking a financial and legal hit.

However, the sale only “completes” once payment for the property is made and the buyer finally gets their hands on the keys to their new home.

The completion date will be included in the contract and should be convenient for everyone involved.

How long between exchange of contracts and completion date?

The time between exchange and completion is typically between one and two weeks. This should give everyone involved enough time to prepare for the move – arranging removals, packing, getting mail redirected, arranging utilities for their new home and so on. You may need to book time off work or arrange for someone to take care of your children or any pets.

However, just how quickly you can get everything wrapped up will depend on the circumstances of both the buyer and the seller, as well as what’s going on further up or down the chain.

The time between exchange of contracts and completion will likely take longer if:

  • The seller hasn’t finalised the purchase of their new home, or needs time to arrange alternative accommodation
  • There’s a renter in the chain who needs to give sufficient notice before they can move – they’ll be wary of handing their notice to their landlord until they know their purchase is definitely going ahead
  • There’s a new-build in the chain that isn’t quite ready for its owners to move in

You may also be able to complete in less than a week in certain circumstances. This might work if:

  • There’s no chain, or it’s short and straightforward
  • You’re moving into a vacant property
  • You’re a first-time buyer and don’t have much to pack
  • You don’t need to give notice to a landlord

Can exchange and completion happen on the same day?

It is possible to exchange and complete in one day. This can be an option for buy-to-let investors and cash buyers, but conveyancing solicitors warn it’s high risk for home movers and people using a mortgage.

“Some lenders will release money with a day’s notice, but others won’t,” says Steph Lyke, of law firm SAS Daniels in Chester. “You’ve got no control over when the funds arrive. On a busy day the Chaps system can be slow.”

This can make for a stressful moving day – especially if you have packed up your belongings and paid for removals without the certainty that the sale and purchase will go ahead. And even if you and your solicitor have all your ducks in a row, you could still face delays if other people in the chain encounter any last-minute problems.

By having a few days, at least, between exchange and completion, the solicitors will have time to deal with any issues that may arise.

Another point to consider is that if you exchange contracts with a future completion date agreed, you could be entitled to compensation if completion ends up being delayed (as it will be a breach of contract). If you exchange and complete on the same day that won’t happen, meaning you could end up out of pocket for any losses you incur as a result.

Delays to completion

The majority of buyers and sellers will complete on the date that they have agreed. However, delays are possible for several reasons, including:

  • Mortgage funds are not received from a mortgage lender in time
  • Help to Buy or lifetime Isa funds do not arrive in time
  • A tenant refuses to move out of a rental property
  • One party in the chain fails to do something that’s required of them in time
  • Administrative errors

What happens on completion day?

This is the day everyone has been holding out for, when the sale is finalised: the seller moves out and the new buyer gets the keys.

For this to happen the funds to purchase the property need to be paid from the buyer’s solicitor to the seller’s solicitor. Only once the money has been sent and received can the ownership of the property be legally transferred to its new owner.

At this point the buyer can collect the keys, usually from the estate agent, and the seller must move out.

Completion typically takes place between 1pm and 2pm, but there may be delays if there’s a lengthy chain.

What is the best day to complete a house sale?

There is no “best” day of the week to complete and move home.

Friday is a popular choice, naturally, as it gives movers the weekend to unpack, potentially reducing the amount of time they need to take off work.

However, that means removal companies are likely to be busier on Fridays. The banking system may also operate more slowly, increasing the risk of delays with those all-important money transfers.

Another issue to consider is conveyancing fraud – where scammers have been known to hack into solicitors’ emails to divert clients’ funds. Due to so many completions taking place on a Friday – and the fact that banks are less equipped to get the money back over the weekend – this has been coined “Friday afternoon fraud”, according to HomeOwners Alliance.

As such, it may be beneficial to move earlier in the week.

Exchange and completion FAQs

Yes, it is still possible for things to go wrong between exchange and completion, but it’s less common than earlier on in the buying process. For example, the chain could collapse if one party is made redundant and their mortgage offer is subsequently withdrawn.

Sometimes communication breakdowns between the buyer and seller can cause problems, particularly if new information emerges or the buyer discovers that the property information form hasn’t been completed accurately.

The conveyancing solicitors involved in a property sale will include the completion date in the contract, however, discussions should take place to agree a date that is convenient for all the buyers and sellers involved.

The risk of a sale falling through decreases substantially once contracts have been exchanged as all parties will have legally committed to the process. However, it is still possible for disaster to strike – somebody in the chain could lose their job, become suddenly ill or even die, meaning the move can’t go ahead. They could pull out for personal reasons too, despite the financial penalty.

Problems could also arise with any property in the chain, for example, structural damage could become apparent or it could suffer a fire, meaning major repairs are required and the sale can’t go ahead.

The number of days it takes lenders to release funds can vary, but typically it takes between three and seven days.

A notice to complete is a legal document that gives the non-ready party a period in order to fulfil their contractual obligations and complete the sale, often around ten days. If the stated period is exceeded, this can allow the non-defaulting party to terminate the contract or even forfeit the deposit if they are the seller. There’s also the potential for them to sue for any damages.

You need buildings insurance from the exchange of contracts, not completion of the sale. From the minute you exchange contracts, the buyer is now legally responsible for the property.

data.title: AI legal start-up backed by Revolut founder chased for unpaid debts
data.description:
A UK artificial intelligence (AI) start-up backed by Revolut founder Nikolay Storonsky has been hit with a winding-up petition from the taxman as it scrambles to secure its future.

Robin AI, a legal technology assistant, is being chased by HMRC for unpaid debts after efforts to raise tens of millions of pounds fell through earlier this year.

HMRC submitted a winding-up petition against the company at the start of November, in a last-ditch attempt to claw back its money.

Winding up petitions are used by creditors to pursue outstanding debts and can ultimately lead to a forced sale of company assets.

It comes as Robin AI seeks to complete an emergency sale to stay afloat, with several parties understood to have submitted offers ahead of a deadline last Thursday.

Robin AI is one of a clutch of UK legal start-ups to have emerged in recent years, bankrolled by venture capitalists seeking to cash in on breakthroughs in technology.

Legal AI technology can speed up document reviews and help draft contracts. However, Robin AI also offers a chatbot to help answer solicitors’ questions while it analyses documents.

Promise around its technology led to it being backed by Mr Storonsky, tech giant PayPal, Singaporean fund Temasek and venture firm Episode 1.

However, like other early-stage businesses, it has burned through millions of pounds and remains loss-making.

The company was put up for sale earlier this year after a planned $50m (£38m) funding round fell through.

Documents prepared for the sale process, dubbed Project Hoodie, show it had around £3.6m of cash as of June from annual revenues of £7.4m.

Investors have ploughed hundreds of billions of dollars into AI software in the hope that the technology can transform the workplace and society.

However, some experts have raised concerns that many AI projects have so far failed to pay dividends.

One study by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology claimed that 95pc of AI pilots failed to generate a return for businesses.

Richard Robinson, Robin AI’s chief executive, declined to comment.

An HMRC spokesman said it could not comment on individual cases. The spokesman said it only issued court actions after it had “exhausted all other options”.

data.title: The existential threat gnawing at the heart of the European economy
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s frustration, bordering on outright anger, is palpable.

The boss of Ineos, one of Europe’s leading chemicals companies, can only look on helplessly as his various business interests are crucified by a combination of crippling energy costs and aggressive, state-subsidised, Chinese competition.

Well, perhaps not quite helpless. Over the past week, he has filed an additional 10 applications for anti-dumping measures with the European Commission in the hope of partially neutering the Chinese onslaught.

But the EU grinds exceedingly slowly and legalistically on such matters, and even if it upholds the complaints, action is likely to be more than a year away. This is possibly too late for an industry already struggling to keep its head above water.

Nor is it just chemicals. Across the board of heavy industry, high-end engineering, telecoms equipment, the components needed for renewable energy, and much else besides, Britain and Europe are facing an existential crisis.

“Unless Europe wakes up fast, it won’t just lose its chemical industry – it will lose the foundation of its entire manufacturing sector,” Tom Crotty, Ineos group director, said this week.

And there’s the truth. British and European policy makers are only too aware of the threat – or at least claim to be – but they also seem quite incapable of doing anything substantive about it.

The UK economy might seem better placed to withstand the onslaught than much of the rest of Europe, if only because it has already been through much of its de-industrialisation phase and is today an overwhelmingly service-based economy.

The problem is much worse in Germany and other parts of central Europe, heavily reliant as they still are on manufacturing prowess to support jobs and keep the wheels of commerce turning.

Yet even in Britain, the scars are already obvious – with rising unemployment and job openings still woefully short of pre-pandemic levels.

Britain’s headline unemployment rate at 5pc might not seem so bad, but it gives a wholly misleading impression of what’s really happening in the labour market.

Add in those of working age not looking for employment, but supported instead by welfare, and it is clear that Britain has a major problem with wider joblessness. This may in part be caused by the lasting impact on employment of the “China shock”.

Much has been written and said about “de-globalisation” since Donald Trump launched his “liberation day” tariffs in April. But it has all been water off a duck’s back for China, which continues to globalise its excess production regardless.

Anything blocked by the US is merely diverted elsewhere.

Chinese chemical imports to the EU surged 8.3pc in the first half of this year. It’s much the same story with Chinese cars, machine tools, solar panels, and wind turbines.

There can be no complaint if the competition is free and fair. But we are a long way from that.

According to data collated by the International Monetary Fund, industrial subsidies in China are running at well in excess of 4pc of GDP, and a lot more taking account of the effective energy subsidy relative to most European economies implicit in continued, high levels of carbon-based electricity generation.

It would make obvious sense for China to instead spend all of this money on improved social safety nets, so as to help rebalance the economy away from investment and export-led growth towards more sustainable domestic consumption.

But this assumes that the presiding purpose of industrial policy in China is the economic well-being of citizens.

Alas, the greater imperative seems to be global domination in key industries and supply chains – and perhaps more malevolently still – the impoverishment of Western democracies.

Hardest hit by China’s latest export drive is Germany. Once the unchallenged superpower of European industry, Germany only has itself to blame for its current predicament.

Like China, it deliberately prioritised export-led growth over domestic consumption, and to begin with at least, grew fat on Chinese demand for its cars, machine tools and its wide range of other high-end manufactured goods.

But now the boot is on the other foot. China has begun to excel in all these things, and with demand at home struggling amid a devastating property crash, is dumping them on Western markets instead.

What happened with solar panels, where once dominant and world-leading German technology has been largely wiped out by aggressive, state-subsidised expansion of Chinese production, is now happening to other industries as well.

In a previously almost unimaginable development, Germany recently began to run a trade deficit in capital goods with China. In capital goods, for heaven’s sake, once the very backbone of German industrial might.

German policymakers are loath to act because they are still living in the past, and hope to resume trade with China on mutually beneficial terms.

With the economy flatlining for the fourth year in a row, you might think that by now they would have learnt their lesson. But still, there is an overriding reluctance to risk giving China offence.

It’s a similar story here in Britain, where punishing energy costs further tilt the playing field away from UK producers and where the default setting in relations with China is one of appeasement.

In a paper published last month, the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero predicted a clean energy jobs boom – with more than 400,000 extra jobs created in Britain’s energy transition by the end of the decade.

This may or may not come true, though for choice I’d put it into the same basket as Ed Miliband’s promise to reduce household energy bills by £300, also by the end of the decade. Given current policy settings, this is about as likely as a month of Sundays.

And if it happens at all, it will be down not to clean energy, but the emerging glut in supplies of liquefied natural gas – which is driving the overall cost of energy down.

In any case, Miliband now promises “a pro-worker, pro-jobs, pro-union, agenda that will deliver the national renewal our country needs”.

All of which is great news for China, which is now almost completely dominant in solar energy and also increasingly monopolises the components needed for wind.

Today’s energy transition may indeed create a lot of jobs in assembly, installation and maintenance. But the way things are, it’ll be largely based on Chinese kit.

Whatever happened to all that starry-eyed talk of a decarbonising renaissance in British manufacturing?

I’m not a great fan of Trump’s protectionism, but he’s right about one thing. For security reasons, if none other, a high degree of local production has to be maintained.

And if that involves playing the Chinese at their own game – with high barriers to entry or by limiting market access to those who establish local sources of production and to joint ventures with local partners, so be it.

As it is, Western economies are writing their own epitaph.
