data.title: The residents charged £4,400 a year for leaf collection
data.description:
Struggling with health issues and worried about paying her mortgage, the last thing Pauline Brown needed was more bad news.

But when her service charge jumped overnight from £11 to £98 a month – equivalent to £1,176 a year – she was left at a complete loss.

With her sick pay from work at just over £400 a month, she simply couldn’t afford to cover the fee.

Brown, who owns the freehold of her house, which lies on the 173-home Ribble Road estate in south Liverpool, approached Plus Dane Housing, the housing association that manages the land, to ask why she has seen such a steep increase in price. She had lived in the property since 1986 and had only ever seen moderate annual rises – nothing close to the near-800pc rise now being demanded.

The company told her it was linked to the rising cost of living and covered grounds maintenance for the estate, including £5,150 for weed control, £5,500 for grass cutting and £4,400 for leaf collection.

All the charges had jumped by exactly 16.49pc from the year before.

“I said, ‘So £98 a month for that? Well, I’m sorry, I’m not paying it,” she says.

Plus Dane Housing insisted that Brown was responsible for one-19th of the estate, as outlined in the original transfer deeds for her house, and that she therefore had no choice but to stump up.

She has stood her ground, but now has a £1,000 debt looming over her. “It’s always in the back of my mind even though I try not to think about it,” she says.

It comes as the service charge system in Britain falls under growing scrutiny.

Labour is planning a draft bill to strengthen the rights of both freeholders and leaseholders amid a rise in complaints from residents up and down the country. It includes making charges more transparent and easier to challenge cost increases.

Suzanne Mana, co-founder of the Social Housing Action Campaign group, says the scale of issues concerning service charges is “absolutely massive”.

“If you talk to MPs, a lot will tell you that their inboxes are absolutely crammed with similar cases,” she says.

Under the current system, freeholders have no implied “test of reasonableness” to fall back on for service charges. This is unlike leaseholders, who are protected under the Landlord and Tenant Act.

It means fees can be raised at will without consultation, while any dispute has to go through the county court. Freeholders have no statutory rights to see any documentary evidence relating to charges, but are obliged to pay as typically outlined in the onerous deeds of the properties.

Halima Ali, of freeholder campaign group HorNet, claims it effectively gives housing associations and estate management companies “a licence to print money”.

“We’re not getting anything out of this apart from being cash cows. It’s soul-destroying, and we’re powerless to do anything,” she says.

Between 2019 and 2024, average service charges increased by a third, with the North West recording the second-largest rise at 57.6pc – well above the average inflation rate of 2.5pc.

At the same time, salaries at housing associations have risen.

Ian Reed, the chief executive of Plus Dane Housing, which manages more than 13,500 homes in the North West, saw his annual pay rise from £182,000 in 2023 to £214,000 last year. Plus Dane Housing says Mr Reed’s salary increase reflects the fact that he had not received a pay rise since 2020.

Meanwhile, Pauline Brown is just one of a score of homeowners on the Ribble Road estate who have watched their service charge skyrocket in recent months – in one case by more than 1,100pc.

Victoria Rooney, another homeowner on the estate, was left shell-shocked after being told the bill for leaf collection was rising from £3,790 a year to £4,400.

“We’re not talking expansive landscaping here at all. I’ve counted, and there are about nine trees.”

Despite charges for litter picking rising to nearly £1,000 a year, the condition of the estate has declined, according to several residents.

“A couple of months ago, there was abandoned rubbish and trolleys and all kinds. It was just in a terrible state,” says Christina Black, whose service charge jumped from £6 to £18 after Plus Dane Housing said it had been charging her “incorrectly” in the past.

Black, who has lived on the estate for 40 years, has been battling cancer and says the whole experience has added undue stress.

“Last winter, I was really unwell, and I really didn’t know whether I was going to pull through. It took its toll on me.”

As well as fears about running up a large debt, there are also concerns that the higher charges could be affecting the value of homes on the estate.

“I know for a fact people have been selling their house, and the buyers have backed out because they said, ‘Well, we’re paying this service charge, but for what?’” says Anne, who also saw her bill triple overnight.

“I don’t mind paying a small fee if the place is kept decent.”

Plus Dane Housing says it has “dealt with several concerns and complaints in relation to the services being provided at Ribble Road” and has “taken some steps to improve them”.

They add: “Our grounds maintenance service visits 16 times per year and includes lawn mowing, hedge trimming, litter picking and leaf collection during the visits between mid-September and March.”

Claire Dixon, deputy chief executive for Plus Dane Housing, defends the inconsistency of service charges, saying the company has “a different legal relationship and a different set of obligations to meet for each customer”.

She says that Plus Dane Housing complies “with all statutory requirements for rent and service charges, which includes ensuring sufficient notice periods for informing customers of any changes” and “ensures customers only pay for the services related to that estate”.

Plus Dane Housing says while there have been general inflationary costs, “there are also a number of residents that have had their charges adjusted in line with their legal agreement, as was the case in the largest increase on the estate from £7 to £87”.

‘The Government needs to sort this’

Britain’s convoluted leasehold and freehold system has been a target for government reform for years.

In its manifesto, Labour pledged to take action to tackle “unfair maintenance costs” and give freeholders more rights to challenge service charges through tribunals.

However, although the Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act was passed in May last year, only a limited number of its provisions have been brought into effect under the current Government.

Ali, of Hornet, estimates that around three million freeholders are affected by burdensome service charges. She is worried that Labour’s pledge to build 1.5 million new homes by the end of this Parliament will leave millions more trapped.

“The Government is constantly pushing this ‘build, baby, build’ mantra, and we’re constantly saying, ‘You need to sort this issue out before you build more homes,’” she says.

Last year, a report by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) identified issues with “high and unclear” estate management charges.

It subsequently recommended a policy of “adoption” in which councils, rather than private companies, oversee amenities on all new housing estates.

But Ali believes the policy needs to encompass all privately managed estates, both new and old, to make sure nobody is left behind.

“A lot of these people are already struggling,” she says. “This is why we need universal adoption. To help all of us who are already trapped.”

In a separate case, a parliamentary enquiry was held last week amid concerns about maintenance issues, missing reserve funds and opaque fees at FirstPort, the UK’s largest leasehold management company.

MPs heard evidence relating to alleged failures to carry out essential work, poor communication and a lack of transparency over service charge calculations and insurance costs.

A FirstPort spokesman said: “We were not given the opportunity to participate in this meeting, but we have worked closely with MPs to resolve customer enquiries and keep them updated on the changes we’re making across our business.

“This includes continued improvements of our systems, ways of working, and customer service, and making sure our teams have the right skills and qualifications.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said it was “already consulting on detailed plans to drive up transparency of service charges” to “make it easier for leaseholders to challenge unreasonable bills”.

data.title: ‘Cash-in-hand barbers are an attack on businesses like ours’
data.description:
With just over two weeks to go until the most anticipated Budget in recent memory, workers and businesses of all stripes are clamouring for some reprieve from Rachel Reeves.

The Chancellor hammered employers last year with a rise in National Insurance contributions, dealing a £23bn blow to the economy, and adding 2pc to employers’ payroll costs.

But Britain’s hairdressers argue they face an existential threat posed by the spread of cash-in-hand barbershops, made worse by a tax burden that is three times the size of a typical high street business.

“We have 16 barbershops in our area now, and I don’t know how they’ve managed to pay the high rent for the high street premises,” says James Talbot, who has run barbershops in Oxfordshire for 13 years.

Many barbershops operate in ways designed to minimise tax, including by disguising employment by classifying workers as self-employed when they are effectively employees. This set-up allows business to limit their profits to stay under the VAT threshold and dodge higher tax bills.

A report by the British Hair Consortium and CBI Economics found that a so-called “disguised employment business” contributed less than half the tax of a traditional employer business.

Local Data Company figures show 18,642 barbershops were operating in the UK as of 2024, with hundreds more opening each year despite the relative decline of the high street in big cities and market towns.

Self-employment in the hairdressing and beauty industry rose from 46pc in 2006 to 63pc in 2024. By 2028, nine in 10 hairdressers will be self-employed, CBI Economics said.

Traditional hairdressers point to a growing hidden economy where substantial income is going untaxed, giving an unfair edge to cash-in-hand businesses, which can charge lower prices.

Richard Tice, deputy leader of Reform UK, vowed last year to crack down on barbershops, dubbing the cash-in-hand businesses “another great British taxpayer rip-off”.

Earlier this year HMRC and the National Crime Agency’s “Operation Machinize” raided more than 250 barbershops, vape shops and nail bars across England and Wales suspected of underpaying tax.

The raids also suggested some barbershops served as fronts for criminal activity, including the sale of illicit vapes.

Those prosecuted included Omead Ibrahim Abdullah Khoshnaw, who traded as Jimmy’s Barbers in Sunderland.

Khoshnaw was fined £35,100 after HMRC ruled he had defaulted on £50,100 of tax between 2016 and 2023. The business is no longer trading.

“My understanding if you’re taking cash only, that’s an opportunity not to declare turnover. We have a card machine – all our turnover gets recognised,” says Talbot. “It’s unfair on us and our staff – and it’s unfair on society.

“We want to encourage entrepreneurs to contribute admirably to our failing society. We have no money in the system because lots of businesses are consciously keeping themselves below the £90,000 threshold.”

In addition to competitors that can undercut their prices, hairdressers face a high tax burden compared to a typical high street business.

In an example given by the CBI, a salon with an annual turnover of £1.2m would pay £80,000 more in VAT and £480,000 more in labour costs than an equivalent high street retail business.

Salons’ tax bills are so high because around 60pc of their turnover goes to paying staff, compared to about 12pc for a typical high street business.

Selling haircuts means almost every pound a salon earns is taxed more – first by labour taxes, such as NI and pensions, then by VAT.

However, while retail and hospitality businesses mostly pay VAT, they can claim much of it back because they buy goods, so they’re taxed less on business inputs.

“The playing field is already vastly unlevel,” says Toby Dicker, of the Hair Consortium. “Roughly 60pc of our costs go on labour – five times the amount of other businesses. We’re the hardest hit on the high street.”

Industry bodies are calling on Ms Reeves to lower the VAT threshold for salons to “level the playing field” with other businesses. Dicker says Labour is sympathetic to the idea, even as the Treasury mulls a manifesto-breaching rise in income tax to balance the books.

The CBI Economics report also warned that the sector’s capacity to offer training for young workers was in critical decline. The report estimated that by 2027, the number of apprenticeship starts was projected to reach zero, costing £3.2bn in productivity benefits.

“We’re unable to take on the youngsters we want to take on,” says Talbot of his Oxfordshire salons. “We want to see our trade evolve, but it’s losing its trendy and fashionable edge if the area is filled with barbershops.”

The Treasury was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Asda market share hits record low as supermarket’s sales slide
data.description:
Asda’s share of the grocery market has plunged to a new record low, piling pressure on boss Allan Leighton to show signs of a turnaround after a year in charge.

The supermarket’s sales slump deepened in the 12-week period to Nov 2 in what was the 20th consecutive month of decline, according to Worldpanel figures.

Sales tumbled by 3.9pc, compared to a 3.2pc drop in the prior period. Asda’s share of the grocery market slipped to 11.8pc from 12.7pc a year ago when Mr Leighton took charge.

It means Asda’s market share is at the lowest level since Worldpanel started collecting data in 2011. Its market share was 14.8pc when the supermarket was taken over by private equity firm TDR Capital and brothers Mohsin and Zuber Issa in 2021.

The decline is a major blow to Mr Leighton, who has promised to revive the struggling grocer after taking over as chief executive last November.

Mr Leighton, who previously ran Asda in the late 1990s, had said his revival drive would take at least three years, likening it to “climbing Everest”.

Since the start of the year, Mr Leighton has pushed through a series of price cuts and spent more on improving the availability of products in stores.

However, the latest figures raise questions over whether he is doing enough to put Asda back on the right track.

Clive Black, an analyst at Shore Capital, says he is surprised that “Asda’s trading is not in a much more stable place, which suggests considerable brand damage in recent years”.

In particular, Asda’s major price-cutting drive – which kicked off at the start of the year with the relaunch of its “Rollback” programme – would have been expected to start winning customers back.

Mr Black says it is not clear whether Asda “underestimated the competitor response”.

“If Asda does not show greater signs of trading stability soon, notably after the anniversary of the introduction of RollBack, then there will be elevated grounds for concern,” he said.

It is not just that Asda is struggling to stem falling sales; customers are also taking a dimmer view of the supermarket.

In Sainsbury’s interim results last week, the grocer highlighted recent survey figures from YouGov, which it claimed showed that Asda ranked well behind competitors on customers’ price perception.

For Mr Leighton, it thrusts a fresh spotlight on what he is doing to improve performance.

Much of the recent focus has involved getting staff on board with his strategy, with the executive taking an unconventional approach to win over employees.

Last month, Asda hosted a series of team-building days at its head office for which it built a faux “base camp” of Everest, including erecting a rock-climbing structure in its car park for staff to climb.

A spokesman for Asda said: “At the start of the year, we set out a strategy to re-establish Asda as the UK’s cheapest traditional supermarket, improve product availability and enhance the customer experience.

“We’ve made solid progress across all areas, particularly on price, where we are now significantly cheaper than competitors’ base and loyalty prices, despite rising food price inflation. We recognise it will take time to rebuild market share, but we remain confident in the strength of our customer proposition going forward.”

data.title: Asda market share hits record low as supermarket’s sales slide
data.description:
Asda’s share of the grocery market has plunged to a new record low, piling pressure on boss Allan Leighton to show signs of a turnaround after a year in charge.

The supermarket’s sales slump deepened in the 12-week period to Nov 2 in what was the 20th consecutive month of decline, according to Worldpanel figures.

Sales tumbled by 3.9pc, compared to a 3.2pc drop in the prior period. Asda’s share of the grocery market slipped to 11.8pc from 12.7pc a year ago when Mr Leighton took charge.

It means Asda’s market share is at the lowest level since Worldpanel started collecting data in 2011. Its market share was 14.8pc when the supermarket was taken over by private equity firm TDR Capital and brothers Mohsin and Zuber Issa in 2021.

data.title: EasyJet under pressure as Jet2 launches flights from Gatwick
data.description:
EasyJet faces a challenge to its dominance at Gatwick Airport after discount rival Jet2 revealed that it would launch flights there.

Jet2, based in Leeds and already a force at northern airports such as Manchester, will begin services from the London hub in March after securing scarce operating slots there.

Steve Heapy, its chief executive, said flights from Gatwick will mark a “transformational next step in Jet2’s ambitions”, opening up a market of 15 million people within an hour’s travel.

EasyJet shares fell 3pc in early trading following news of the plan, while Jet2 stock traded higher.

The launch will establish Jet2 as a major operator at Gatwick, with the airport’s chief executive, Pierre-Hugues Schmit, saying Jet2 will be the biggest new entrant at the airport this century.

The arrival of Jet2 at Britain’s second-busiest airport will represent a particular threat to easyJet’s holiday business, which has become a key profit driver in recent years.

While easyJet has been selling an increasing number of hotel and flight packages, Jet2 is by far the largest tour operator in the UK, with seven million package customers this year. That’s more than twice the number at easyJet.

The first summer of Jet2’s Gatwick operations will feature flights to 29 resorts in countries including Spain, Greece, Turkey, Portugal and Italy. The inaugural service will be to Tenerife on March 26, ahead of the Easter holidays.

The heightened competition comes amid concerns that tax increases in the upcoming Budget may force Britons to rein in holiday spending.

Jet2’s expansion coincides with Gatwick moving forward with a £2.2bn second runway, after the Government approved the project in September.

The new landing strip will almost double Gatwick’s capacity to around 80 million passengers a year, allowing airlines to add 110,000 more flights. The second runway could open as early as 2030.

Jet2 was able to get into Gatwick after the release of a batch of new flight slots, which Mr Heapy called a “once in a generation opportunity”. He said that Jet2, the third-largest UK airline by passenger numbers, had long harboured ambitions to operate from the southern airport.

Six Airbus planes will initially serve Gatwick, which Jet2 said will create more than 300 local jobs, including pilots, engineers and cabin crew. The launch is a boost for Gatwick, which lost some major carriers, including Virgin Atlantic, during the Covid pandemic.

data.title: Forced out of retirement by a £25k bill: MPs probe freeholder over service charges
data.description:
Leaseholders from across Britain have been called to give evidence to MPs as part of a campaign to hold managing agents to account for alleged failings.

Twelve residents last week spoke on a range of alleged issues experienced under the management of FirstPort, the UK’s largest leasehold management company. These include escalating charges, poor service quality, opaque fees and a lack of communication.

Rachel Blake, Labour MP for Cities of London and Westminster, said she had “heard enough excuses” from FirstPort, after Martin King, the company’s managing director, pledged to make improvements to operations at a meeting with MPs in January.

“FirstPort must now demonstrate proper accountability,” she said. “Leaseholders deserve transparency and timely repairs.”

Currently, England and Wales’s five million leaseholders are required to pay a service charge to cover the upkeep of shared areas in their building and grounds – at an average of £2,300 per year.

French-owned FirstPort manages around 300,000 properties across Britain, with more than one in 60 under its stewardship.

But in the past year, it has fallen under growing scrutiny as part of a wider review of the UK’s service charge system – described by Labour MP Justin Madders as the “PPI of the housebuilding industry” for the scale of alleged misconduct.

Facebook group Firstport Resident’s Action Group has 13,500 members and receives dozens of claims every week about issues such as delays to urgent repairs and missing reserve funds.

‘Horrendous since day one’

Chia Yen Oh, who attended the parliamentary inquiry session on behalf of a housing estate in Colchester, Essex, claimed she had been left feeling anxious and stressed by her attempts to challenge FirstPort’s raising of charges and alleged poor service provision.

“It has been horrendous since day one,” she said. “It’s just always niggling. I’m a single mum, I’ve got two part-time jobs and a business and I have to do this on top of it. It’s very time-consuming and draining.”

Sarah Colvin, whose housing estate in Nottingham was represented by Louise Scull at the inquiry, said her husband, Ray, had been forced out of retirement after FirstPort neglect left them facing a £25,000 bill for repairs on their Grade II listed block of apartments in Edwalton Hall.

After their freeholder got rid of FirstPort following complaints about a lack of maintenance, the new managing agent said it would cost around £250,000 between 10 flats to restore the block. This was in spite of paying thousands every year to FirstPort for the upkeep of the building and grounds, and continuing to pay around £4,000 a year in fees.

“It’s going to knock our life savings out of kilter – £25,000 upfront is a great deal of money to find. We have five grandchildren and we were hoping we’d be able to help ease their way at university.

“My husband is 75 and he’s back to working as hard as he ever did.”

Ms Colvin said a separate issue involving a three-month delay fixing a lift in the building also left an elderly resident “effectively housebound”.

‘Power imbalance’

In a House of Commons debate on the topic last Thursday, Conservative MP Rebecca Paul said issues concerning FirstPort at a housing estate in her Surrey constituency were “symptomatic of an industry that prioritises extracting maximum value from leaseholders regardless of the human cost”.

“They are opportunists making money from an inequitable system,” she said. “This fundamental power imbalance must be addressed. It must be easier for leaseholders to take their business elsewhere.”

Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, said he would request a meeting with Mr King to address concerns directly.

In 2024, average service charges rose by 11pc, well above the inflation rate of 2.5pc.

A rise in complaints about soaring fees and poor quality work has prompted cross-party MPs to summon managing agents, including FirstPort, RMG, and Trinity to Parliament for questioning.

The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act passed in the final days of the Conservative government aimed to address some of the common issues concerning service charges.

These include giving leaseholders more transparency about what their fees are being spent on, as well as making it easier to challenge any increases.

But MPs have called for more radical reforms that would see local authorities “adopt” communal lands and infrastructure on the completion of new estates, thus taking it out of the hands of management companies.

MP Fleur Anderson criticised the Leasehold Act, calling it “rushed and poorly drafted” and said it failed to ban new leasehold homes.

James Naish, MP for Rushcliffe, the constituency in which Ms Colvin and her husband live, said the Government needed to tackle issues around leasehold management companies before it embarked on a building blitz.

“We’ve got to get the long-term reform right,” he said. “If we’re going to be building a million or more homes, there needs to be the right legislative framework in place, instead of creating more of these problems.”

Dozens of MPs have written to FirstPort after listening to complaints from constituents, outlining specific complaints and making fresh demands to improve their service with a timeline for tangible improvement.

A spokesman for FirstPort said: “We conduct regular inspections across all the communal areas we manage and work closely with specialist contractors to deliver tailored maintenance programmes. However, issues can occasionally arise, particularly in older buildings.

“As soon as we became aware of the water ingress at Edwalton Hall, we began investigating the repair works needed. Responsibility for completing these works has since been handed over to the new managing agent. While we have not seen the full scope of the planned repairs, our experience shows that costs associated with Grade II listed buildings can be considerably higher.

“As a business, we have made significant improvements to our systems, ways of working and customer service, and we are also continuing to invest in our people, ensuring they have the right skills and qualifications.”

data.title: Has the BBC just scuppered its chance to secure its future?
data.description:
When the BBC rolled out a new subscription service in the US in June, bosses hailed a “major milestone” that would “unlock new opportunities for growth”.

The decision to put most of its online news behind a paywall was driven by a desire to generate more revenue on the other side of the Atlantic as audiences at home increasingly shunned the licence fee.

It was a fine idea – but there was a problem. Behind the scenes, bosses were already grappling with complaints of bias that would ultimately explode into the open and trigger the corporation’s most acute crisis in at least a decade.

The same month as the subscription service launched, Michael Prescott, the author of a devastating dossier exposing bias in the BBC’s reporting, stepped down as an adviser to the broadcaster’s editorial standards committee.

The Telegraph’s subsequent exposé of his report has resulted in the resignation of Tim Davie, the director-general, as well as Deborah Turness, the head of news.

The fallout quickly spread to the US. One of the key charges was that the BBC displayed anti-Trump bias in its edit of a speech made by the president on the day of the Jan 6 2021 Capitol riots. Donald Trump has now threatened to sue the BBC for $1bn (£760m), railing against what he claims are the “corrupt” journalists who oversaw a misleading edit of his speech.

Even if he fails to follow through on his threat, the president has the power to make the BBC’s stateside life very difficult indeed. The crisis threatens to derail the BBC’s American push before it has even got off the ground.

Bosses in W1A initially looked to the US because of the sorry state of the public service broadcaster’s finances.

Its income from the licence fee has declined by 30pc in real terms over the last 15 years thanks to a lethal combination of government-ordered freezes and declining viewership. Last year, a further 300,000 households stopped paying the licence fee, which will soon rise above £180.

This has left the BBC with a funding shortfall of almost £500m, prompting successive rounds of gruelling cuts to both staff and programming.

The broadcaster has staked its hopes of recovery on BBC Studios, the commercial arm that generates revenues by selling shows and licencing its brands around the world.

There have been tentative signs that this strategy is paying off. BBC Studios pulled in record revenues of £2.2bn in the year to the end of March, up by £300m on the previous year. It remains on track to hit its target of returning £1.5bn to the licence fee payer by March 2027 – a 30pc increase on the previous five-year period.

Much of this success can be pinned on Bluey, the hit children’s show that took the crown of most streamed programme in the US in 2024.

The BBC has sought to double down on this streaming success. In late 2024, it sold its stake in TV channel BBC America to partner AMC and moved to take full control of streaming platform BritBox International after buying out ITV’s share for £255m.

BritBox’s international revenues rose by a fifth in the BBC’s first year of full ownership, driven by the popularity of shows such as Ludwig and Blue Lights with North American viewers. US subscribers pay $10.99 a month.

The news paywall, meanwhile, means people in the US have to pay $8.99 a month to access most BBC News stories and stream the channel. The corporation says its website reaches almost 60 million visitors in the US, though it has not yet revealed how many have taken up subscriptions.

It has not all been plain sailing. When, in 2022, the BBC unveiled a partnership with Disney to stream Doctor Who internationally, it held up the deal – worth an estimated $100m – as a prime example of how it would take British ideas and sell them abroad.

Yet in an embarrassing climbdown last month, the broadcaster was forced to admit that the House of Mouse had pulled out of the partnership after just two series following reports of disappointing viewing figures.

Alex DeGroote, a media analyst, warns that the BBC should not be “over optimistic” about what it can achieve in the US – the home of Netflix, Disney, Paramount, Warner Bros and many more media goliaths.

“There is a big market in the US but it’s saturated and very competitive ... The commercial revenue will be additive and can be meaningful, but it will not substitute the perpetuity value of the BBC licence fee,” he says.

“The issue they’ve got is that there’s a growing number of Brits who don’t want to pay for it and who don’t believe in universality, and who don’t like all this sort of wokey stuff.”

The biggest barrier to the BBC’s expansion hopes could ultimately be the president.

Lawyers for Trump this week lambasted the corporation over its “reckless disregard for truth” after a Panorama episode spliced different parts of his speech to wrongly suggest he had incited his supporters to violence. Trump has threatened to sue the BBC for $1bn if it does not issue an apology by Friday.

The US president has a history of suing media organisations, often successfully.

Paramount, the US owner of Channel 5, this year agreed to pay $16m to settle a lawsuit over an interview broadcast with Trump’s election rival Kamala Harris on CBS News. Trump claimed the interview was selectively edited in her favour.

The CBS settlement came shortly after Disney’s ABC paid $15m to settle a defamation case brought by the president.

Trump is currently suing Rupert Murdoch’s News UK for $10bn over allegations published in the Wall Street Journal about his links to Jeffrey Epstein, while the president is seeking $15bn in damages from the New York Times over its coverage of his 2024 election campaign.

But Trump’s ability to damage the BBC is not limited to lawsuits. The US president has recently been accused of exerting influence over the US media through the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), which is run by his acolyte Brendan Carr.

The media regulator was accused by Democrats of engaging in an “unprecedented and illegal campaign to chill free speech” over its handling of the $8bn takeover of Paramount by David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry. Democrats said the settlement of the CBS lawsuit just ahead of the FCC’s approval of the deal “looks like bribery”.

Carr also threatened to revoke ABC’s broadcasting licence over comments made by Jimmy Kimmel about the killing of Charlie Kirk. Disney, which owns the channel, briefly pulled the late night host off air in response.

While the BBC is not regulated by the FCC, this does not mean it cannot wield influence. Unlike Ofcom, the US agency has no qualms about being political.

“I don’t think many Brits will realise but the FCC is quite an active agency,” says DeGroote. “They can make life difficult for the BBC in the US.”

The FCC could, for example, pressure CBS not to work with the BBC. The two broadcasters have had a deal to share news resources since 2017.

CBS presents a headache for the BBC in another way too. While CBS News has traditionally been viewed as Left-leaning, the takeover of parent company Paramount by the Trump-supporting Ellison family has fuelled speculation of a political repositioning.

Bari Weiss, an outspoken “anti-woke” journalist, has been installed as editor-in-chief of CBS News after Paramount bought her online publication The Free Press for $150m.

In an early indication of Weiss’s attitude towards the BBC, The Free Press on Tuesday ran an article by Melanie Phillips, a Times journalist, describing accusations of bias in BBC Arabic’s coverage of Gaza as “wicked, wicked stuff” and arguing that the case for defunding the broadcaster was now “overwhelming”.

Another potential avenue for pressure is the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), the US competition and consumer protection watchdog.

While the BBC’s journalism is beyond the FTC’s jurisdiction, the regulator can investigate the broadcaster’s commercial operations in the States – including BBC.com – for any practices it deems unfair, deceptive or fraudulent to US consumers.

Trump has already cemented his control over the FTC. In September, the US Supreme Court gave the president the green light to fire Rebecca Slaughter, one of the two Democratic commissioners he dismissed without cause earlier this year.

Ultimately, though, the US president need not even deploy his watchdogs to create a headache for the BBC.

Karoline Leavitt, the president’s press secretary, this weekend branded the BBC “100pc fake news” and urged Britons to watch GB News instead. The White House is now considering blacklisting BBC reporters from press briefings, as it has previously done with news outlets including the Associated Press.

The crisis at the BBC is made all the more urgent by the fact that negotiations over the future of the licence fee in the next Charter period are now under way. Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, will kick off a “comprehensive” review of the corporation’s future before Christmas.

For years, bosses have been keen to emphasise the successes of the BBC’s commercial operations abroad, insisting they will play a key role in offsetting the decline in licence fee income.

But with the fallout from the BBC bias scandal only just setting in, Auntie’s greatest hope of securing its future may have already been scuppered.

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”
